**Wuhan**[[a]](#cite_note-15) is the capital of [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei"), China. With a population of 13,739,000, it is the most populous city in Hubei and the [seventh-most-populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_China_by_population "List of cities in China by population") in China.[[15]](#cite_note-16) It is also one of China's nine [national central cities](/wiki/National_central_city "National central city").[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Wuhan historically served as a busy city port for [commerce](/wiki/Commerce "Commerce") and trading with some crucial influences on [Chinese history](/wiki/Chinese_history "Chinese history"). The name "Wuhan" came from the city's historical origin from the conglomeration of [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang,_Wuhan "Wuchang, Wuhan"), [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou_District "Hankou District"), and [Hanyang](/wiki/Hanyang_District "Hanyang District"), which are collectively known as the "Three Towns of Wuhan" (武汉三镇). Wuhan lies in the eastern [Jianghan Plain](/wiki/Jianghan_Plain "Jianghan Plain"), at the confluence of the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") river and its largest tributary, the [Han River](/wiki/Han_River_(Hubei) "Han River (Hubei)"), and is known as "Nine Provinces' Thoroughfare" ([九省通衢](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B9%9D%E7%9C%81%E9%80%9A%E8%A1%A2 "wikt:九省通衢")).[[1]](#cite_note-Readmeok_Sina-1) Wuhan was the site of the 1911 [Wuchang Uprising](/wiki/Wuchang_Uprising "Wuchang Uprising") against the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") which [ended 2,000 years of dynastic rule](/wiki/Dynasties_of_China "Dynasties of China"). Wuhan was briefly a capital of China twice, in 1927 under a left wing [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT) [government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Republic_of_China_in_Wuhan "Government of the Republic of China in Wuhan"),[[17]](#cite_note-Remaking_the_Chinese_City-18) and in 1937 as a provisional wartime capital during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[18]](#cite_note-AN_AMERICAN_IN_CHINA:_1936-39_A_Memoir-19)[[19]](#cite_note-Wuhan,_1938-20) In 1938, during the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"), the city was the site of the [Battle of Wuhan](/wiki/Battle_of_Wuhan "Battle of Wuhan"). On December 31, 2019, [SARS-CoV-2](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome_coronavirus_2 "Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2"), a novel coronavirus that later caused the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), was first discovered in Wuhan[[20]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times-21)[[21]](#cite_note-Oxford_University-22) and the city was the location of [the first](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdown_in_China "COVID-19 lockdown in China") [lockdown](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdowns "COVID-19 lockdowns") of the pandemic in January 2020.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-23)

Wuhan is considered the political, economic, financial, commercial, cultural, and educational center of Central China.[[23]](#cite_note-Focus_on_Wuhan,_China-24) It is a major transportation hub, with dozens of railways, roads, and expressways passing through the city and connecting to other major cities.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Because of its key role in domestic transportation, Wuhan is sometimes referred to as "the [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") of China" by foreign sources.[[3]](#cite_note-timemagazine-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Chicago_is_all_over_the_place-4)[[5]](#cite_note-水野幸吉_Mizuno_Kokichi_2014_3-5) The "Golden Waterway" of the Yangtze River and the Han River traverse the urban area and divide Wuhan into the three districts of [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang_District "Wuchang District"), [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou "Hankou"), and [Hanyang](/wiki/Hanyang_District "Hanyang District"). The [Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge") crosses the Yangtze in the city. The [Three Gorges Dam](/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam "Three Gorges Dam"), the [world's largest power station](/wiki/List_of_largest_power_stations "List of largest power stations") in terms of [installed capacity](/wiki/Installed_capacity "Installed capacity"), is located nearby. Historically, Wuhan has suffered risks of flooding,[[25]](#cite_note-26) prompting the government to alleviate the situation by introducing ecologically friendly absorption mechanisms.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

While Wuhan has been a traditional manufacturing hub for decades, it is also one of the areas promoting modern industrial changes in China. Wuhan has three national development zones, four scientific and technological development parks, over 350 research institutes, 1,656 high tech enterprises, numerous enterprise incubators and investments from 230 Fortune Global 500 firms.[[27]](#cite_note-28) It produced [GDP (nominal)](/wiki/GDP_(nominal) "GDP (nominal)") of US$318 billion in 2025. The [Dongfeng Motor Corporation](/wiki/Dongfeng_Motor_Corporation "Dongfeng Motor Corporation"), an automobile manufacturer, is headquartered in Wuhan. The city is home to [multiple notable institutes](/wiki/List_of_universities_and_colleges_in_Hubei "List of universities and colleges in Hubei") of higher education, including [Wuhan University](/wiki/Wuhan_University "Wuhan University")[[28]](#cite_note-29) and the [Huazhong University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Huazhong_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Huazhong University of Science and Technology"). Wuhan is a major city in the world by scientific research outputs and it ranks [8th](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output "List of cities by scientific output") globally and 5th in the Asia-Pacific (after [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") and [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou")).[[29]](#cite_note-30) In 2017, Wuhan was designated as a [Creative City](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), in the field of design.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Wuhan is classified as a Beta- (global second tier) city together with seven other cities in China, including [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha "Changsha"), [Dalian](/wiki/Dalian "Dalian"), [Jinan](/wiki/Jinan "Jinan"), [Shenyang](/wiki/Shenyang "Shenyang"), [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an") and [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou") by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[31]](#cite_note-32) Wuhan is also one of the world's top 100 financial centers, according to the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[32]](#cite_note-GFCI-33)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name "Wuhan" comes from the two major cities on the banks of the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") that make up the Wuhan metropolis: "Wu" refers to the city of [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang "Wuchang") ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 武昌), which lies on the southern bank of the Yangtze, while "Han" refers to the city of [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou "Hankou") ([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 汉口), which lies on the northern bank of the Yangtze. "Hankou" means "Mouth of the Han", from its position at the confluence of the Han with the Yangtze River.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1926, the [Northern Expedition](/wiki/Northern_Expedition "Northern Expedition") reached the Wuhan area and it was decided to merge Hankou, Wuchang and [Hanyang](/wiki/Hanyang_District "Hanyang District") into one city in order to make a new capital for Nationalist China. On January 1, 1927,[[33]](#cite_note-history1-34) the resulting city was proclaimed as '[武漢](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AD%A6%E6%BC%A2 "wikt:武漢")' (the traditional Chinese characters for 'Wuhan'), which was later [simplified](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") as '武汉' (also 'Wuhan').[[34]](#cite_note-历史沿革-35)[[35]](#cite_note-江汉综述-36)[[36]](#cite_note-武汉的由来-37)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Wuhan](/wiki/History_of_Wuhan "History of Wuhan")

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Erligang_map.svg/250px-Erligang_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Erligang_map.svg)

[Panlongcheng](/wiki/Panlongcheng "Panlongcheng"), located in the southernmost area of the [Erligang culture](/wiki/Erligang_culture "Erligang culture")

The Wuhan area has been settled for 3,500 years. [Panlongcheng](/wiki/Panlongcheng "Panlongcheng"), an archaeological site primarily associated with the [Erligang culture](/wiki/Erligang_culture "Erligang culture") (c. 1510 – c. 1460 BC) (being sparsely populated during the earlier [Erlitou period](/wiki/Erlitou_culture "Erlitou culture")), is located in modern-day [Huangpi District](/wiki/Huangpi_District "Huangpi District") of Wuhan.

During the [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou "Western Zhou") the [State of E](/wiki/E_(state) "E (state)"), which gives its name to the single character abbreviation for Hubei province, controlled the present-day Wuchang area south of the Yangtze River. After the conquest of the E state in 863 BC, the present-day Wuhan area was controlled by the [State of Chu](/wiki/Chu_(state) "Chu (state)") for the rest of the Western Zhou and [Eastern Zhou](/wiki/Eastern_Zhou "Eastern Zhou") periods. After the [State of Huang](/wiki/State_of_Huang "State of Huang") was conquered by State of Chu in the summer of 648 BC,[[37]](#cite_note-38) the people of Huang were moved into the area in and around present-day Wuhan. Local geographical terms including the name of Wuhan's Huangpi District were named after the State of Huang.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Chu was in turn conquered by [Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)") in 223 BC.

### Imperial China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Imperial China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/%E6%AD%A6%E6%B1%89%E9%BB%84%E9%B9%A4%E6%A5%BC.jpg/250px-%E6%AD%A6%E6%B1%89%E9%BB%84%E9%B9%A4%E6%A5%BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A6%E6%B1%89%E9%BB%84%E9%B9%A4%E6%A5%BC.jpg)

[Yellow Crane Tower](/wiki/Yellow_Crane_Tower "Yellow Crane Tower")

During the time of the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), Hanyang became a fairly busy port. The [Battle of Xiakou](/wiki/Battle_of_Xiakou "Battle of Xiakou") in AD 203 and [Battle of Jiangxia](/wiki/Battle_of_Jiangxia "Battle of Jiangxia") five years later were fought in the region over control of [Jiangxia Commandery](/wiki/Jiangxia_Commandery "Jiangxia Commandery"), territories of which included much of present-day eastern Hubei. In the winter of 208/9, one of the most famous battles in [Chinese history](/wiki/Chinese_history "Chinese history") and a central event in the *[Romance of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Romance_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Romance of the Three Kingdoms")*—the [Battle of Red Cliffs](/wiki/Battle_of_Red_Cliffs "Battle of Red Cliffs")—took place near the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"), with the cliffs near Wuhan identified as one of the potential locations.[[38]](#cite_note-:1-39) Around that time, walls were built to protect Hanyang (AD 206) and Wuchang (AD 223). The latter event marks the foundation of Wuhan. In AD 223, the [Yellow Crane Tower](/wiki/Yellow_Crane_Tower "Yellow Crane Tower"), one of the [Four Great Towers of China](/wiki/Four_Great_Towers_of_China "Four Great Towers of China"), was constructed on the Wuchang side of the Yangtze River by order of [Sun Quan](/wiki/Sun_Quan "Sun Quan"), leader of the [Eastern Wu](/wiki/Eastern_Wu "Eastern Wu"). The tower became a sacred site of [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism").[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Due to tensions between the [Eastern Wu](/wiki/Eastern_Wu "Eastern Wu") and [Cao Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei") kingdoms, in the autumn of 228,[[b]](#cite_note-fn1-42) [Cao Rui](/wiki/Cao_Rui "Cao Rui"), grandson of [Cao Cao](/wiki/Cao_Cao "Cao Cao") and the second emperor of the state of [Cao Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei"), ordered the general [Man Chong](/wiki/Man_Chong "Man Chong") to lead troops to Xiakou (夏口; in present-day Wuhan).[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44) In 279, [Wang Jun](/wiki/Wang_Jun_(Jin_dynasty) "Wang Jun (Jin dynasty)") and his army conquered strategic locations in Wu territory such as [Xiling](/wiki/Xiling_District "Xiling District") (in present-day [Yichang](/wiki/Yichang "Yichang"), Hubei), Xiakou (夏口; present-day Hankou) and Wuchang (武昌; present-day [Ezhou](/wiki/Ezhou "Ezhou"), Hubei).

During the [Northern and Southern dynasties](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_dynasties "Northern and Southern dynasties") period, the Wuhan area was part of the successive Southern dynasty states [Liu Song](/wiki/Liu_Song "Liu Song") (420–479), [Southern Qi](/wiki/Southern_Qi "Southern Qi") (479–502), [Liang](/wiki/Liang_dynasty "Liang dynasty"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and [Western Liang](/wiki/Western_Liang_(555%E2%80%93587) "Western Liang (555–587)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In fall 550, [Hou Jing](/wiki/Hou_Jing "Hou Jing") sent Ren Yue to attack both Xiao Daxin and Xiao Fan's son Xiao Si (蕭嗣). Ren killed Xiao Si in battle, and Xiao Daxin, unable to resist, surrendered, allowing Hou to take his domain under control. Meanwhile, Xiao Guan, who had by now settled at Jiangxia (江夏, in modern Wuhan), was planning to attack Hou, but this drew Xiao Yi's ire—believing that Xiao Guan was intending to contend for the throne—and he sent Wang to attack Xiao Guan. In summer 567, Chen Xu commissioned [Wu Mingche](/wiki/Wu_Mingche "Wu Mingche") as the governor of Xiang Province and had him command a major part of the troops against Hua, along with Chunyu Liang (淳于量). The opposing sides met at Zhuankou (沌口, in modern Wuhan).

The city has long been renowned as a center for the arts (especially poetry) and for intellectual studies. [Cui Hao](/wiki/Cui_Hao_(poet) "Cui Hao (poet)"), a celebrated poet of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), visited the Yellow Crane Tower in the early 8th century; his poem made it the most celebrated building in southern China.[[43]](#cite_note-Wan1-45)

In spring 877, [Wang Xianzhi](/wiki/Wang_Xianzhi_(rebel) "Wang Xianzhi (rebel)") captured E Prefecture (鄂州, in modern Wuhan). He then returned north, joining forces with Huang again, and they surrounded Song Wei at Song Prefecture (宋州, in modern [Shangqiu](/wiki/Shangqiu "Shangqiu"), [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan")). In winter 877, [Huang Chao](/wiki/Huang_Chao "Huang Chao") pillaged Qi and Huang (黃州, in modern Wuhan) Prefectures.

Before [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan") arrived in 1259, word reached him that [Möngke](/wiki/M%C3%B6ngke "Möngke") had died. Kublai decided to keep the death of his brother secret and continued the attack on the Wuhan area, near the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze"). The present-day [Wuying Pagoda](/wiki/Wuying_Pagoda "Wuying Pagoda") was constructed at the end of the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") between attacks by the Mongolian forces. Under the [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") rulers ([Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty")) (after 1301), the Wuchang [prefecture](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture"), headquartered in the town, became the capital of [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei") province. Hankou, from the [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") to late [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), was under the administration of the local government in [Hanyang](/wiki/Hanyang_District "Hanyang District"), although it was already one of the four major national markets ([四大名镇](/w/index.php?title=%E5%9B%9B%E5%A4%A7%E5%90%8D%E9%95%87&action=edit&redlink=1 "四大名镇 (page does not exist)")[[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%9B%9B%E5%A4%A7%E5%90%8D%E9%95%87 "zh:四大名镇")]) of the Ming dynasty.

Hanyang's [Guiyuan Temple](/wiki/Guiyuan_Temple "Guiyuan Temple") was completed in the 15th year of Shunzhi (1658).[[44]](#cite_note-46)

By the dawn of the 18th century, Hankou had become one of China's top four [trading](/wiki/Trading "Trading") centers. In the late 19th century, [railroads](/wiki/Railroads "Railroads") were extended on a north–south axis through the city, making Wuhan an important [transshipment](/wiki/Transshipment "Transshipment") point between rail and river traffic. Also during this period foreign powers extracted mercantile concessions, with the riverfront of Hankou being divided up into foreign-controlled merchant districts. These districts contained trading firm offices, warehouses, and docking facilities. The French had a [concession](/wiki/Concession_(territory) "Concession (territory)") in Hankou.[[45]](#cite_note-47) During the [Taiping Heavenly Kingdom](/wiki/Taiping_Heavenly_Kingdom "Taiping Heavenly Kingdom"), the Wuhan area was controlled for many years by rebel forces and the Yellow Crane Tower, [Xingfu Temple](/wiki/Wuying_Pagoda "Wuying Pagoda"), [Zhuodaoquan Temple](/wiki/Zhuodaoquan_Temple "Zhuodaoquan Temple") and other buildings were repurposed or damaged. During the [Second Opium War](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War") (known in the West as the Arrow War, 1856–1860), the government of the Qing dynasty was defeated by the western powers and signed the [Treaties of Tianjin](/wiki/Treaties_of_Tianjin "Treaties of Tianjin") and the [Convention of Peking](/wiki/Convention_of_Peking "Convention of Peking"), which stipulated eleven cities or regions (including Hankou) as trading ports. In December 1858, [James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin](/wiki/James_Bruce,_8th_Earl_of_Elgin "James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin"), High Commissioner to China, led four warships up the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") in Wuhan to collect the information needed for opening the trading port in Wuhan.

In the spring of 1861, Counselor [Harry Smith Parkes](/wiki/Harry_Smith_Parkes "Harry Smith Parkes") and Admiral Herbert were sent to Wuhan to open a trading port. On the basis of the [Convention of Peking](/wiki/Convention_of_Peking "Convention of Peking"), Parkes concluded the Hankou Lend-Lease Treaty with Guan Wen, the governor-general of Hunan and Hubei. It brought an area of 30.53 square kilometers (11.79 sq mi) along the Yangtze River (from latter-day Jianghan Road to Hezuo Road) to become a British Concession and permitted [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") to set up its consulate in the concession.

In 1862, Russian tea merchants arrived in the treaty port of Hankou. Russians in Hankou established four factories using assembly lines and machinery to produce brick tea, and became the city's richest industrialists in what would become the Russian concession.[[46]](#cite_note-:3-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) Japanese immigrants, mainly traders, also started arriving in 1874.[[46]](#cite_note-:3-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Wuhan_1864.jpg/250px-Wuhan_1864.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wuhan_1864.jpg)

Wuhan in 1864

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Hankow_Bund_c._1900.jpg/250px-Hankow_Bund_c._1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hankow_Bund_c._1900.jpg)

Foreign concessions along the [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou "Hankou") Bund c. 1900.

In 1889, [Zhang Zhidong](/wiki/Zhang_Zhidong "Zhang Zhidong") was transferred from [Viceroy of Liangguang](/wiki/Viceroy_of_Liangguang "Viceroy of Liangguang") ([Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") and [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") provinces) to [Viceroy of Huguang](/wiki/Viceroy_of_Huguang "Viceroy of Huguang") ([Hunan](/wiki/Hunan "Hunan") and [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei") provinces). He governed the province for 18 years, until 1907. During this period, he elucidated the theory of "Chinese learning as the basis, Western learning for application," known as the ti-yong ideal. He set up many heavy industries, founded Hanyang Steel Plant, [Daye](/wiki/Huangshi "Huangshi") Iron Mine, Pingxiang Coal Mine and Hubei Arsenal and set up local textile industries, boosting the flourishing modern industry in Wuhan. Meanwhile, he initiated education reform, opened dozens of modern educational organizations successively, such as Lianghu (Hunan and Hubei) Academy of Classical Learning, Civil General Institute, Military General Institute, Foreign Languages Institute and Lianghu (Hunan and Hubei) General Normal School, and selected a great many students for study overseas, which well promoted the development of China's modern education. Furthermore, he trained a modern military and organized a modern army including a *zhen* and a *xie* (both *zhen* and *xie* are military units in the Qing dynasty) in Hubei.

Originally known as the Hubei Arsenal, the [Hanyang Arsenal](/wiki/Hanyang_Arsenal "Hanyang Arsenal") was founded in 1891, with funds diverted from the [Nanyang Fleet](/wiki/Nanyang_Fleet "Nanyang Fleet") in [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") to build the arsenal. It cost about 250,000 pounds sterling and was built in 4 years.[[48]](#cite_note-50) On April 23, 1894, construction was completed and the arsenal, occupying some 40 acres (160,000 m2), could start production of small-caliber cannons. It built magazine-fed rifles, Gruson quick fire guns, and cartridges.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

In 1896, the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") also acquired a concession in Hankou.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

### Wuchang Uprising

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Wuchang Uprising")]

Main article: [Wuchang Uprising](/wiki/Wuchang_Uprising "Wuchang Uprising")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Hankow_1915.jpg/250px-Hankow_1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hankow_1915.jpg)

Map from 1915 showing the location of the three cities ([Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang,_Wuhan "Wuchang, Wuhan"), [Hanyang](/wiki/Hanyang,_Wuhan "Hanyang, Wuhan") and [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou "Hankou")) separated by the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") River and the [Han River](/wiki/Han_River_(Hubei_and_Shaanxi) "Han River (Hubei and Shaanxi)"). The three cities merged in 1927 to form Wuhan.

By 1900, according to [Collier's](/wiki/Collier%27s "Collier's") magazine, Hankou, the Yangtze River boom town, was "the [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") of China."[[4]](#cite_note-Chicago_is_all_over_the_place-4) On October 10, 1911, [Sun Yat-sen](/wiki/Sun_Yat-sen "Sun Yat-sen")'s followers launched the [Wuchang Uprising](/wiki/Wuchang_Uprising "Wuchang Uprising"),[[51]](#cite_note-tonsi86-53) which led to the collapse of the Qing state and 2,000 years of dynastic rule,[[52]](#cite_note-fenby-54) as well as the establishment of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)").[[53]](#cite_note-Welland,_Sasah_Su-ling_2007_pg_87-55)

The Wuchang Uprising of October 1911, which overthrew the Qing dynasty, originated in Wuhan.[[51]](#cite_note-tonsi86-53) Before the uprising, anti-Qing secret societies were active in Wuhan. In September 1911, the outbreak of the protests in Sichuan forced the Qing authorities to send part of the New Army garrisoned in Wuhan to suppress the rebellion.[[54]](#cite_note-Wangke-56) On September 14 the Literary Society (文學社) and the Progressive Association (共進會), two local revolutionary organizations in Hubei,[[54]](#cite_note-Wangke-56) set up joint headquarters in Wuchang and planned for an uprising. On the morning of October 9, a bomb at the office of the political arrangement exploded prematurely and alerted local authorities.[[55]](#cite_note-gongtong6-3-57) The proclamation for the uprising, beadroll and the revolutionaries' official seal fell into the hands of Rui Cheng, the governor-general of Hunan and Hubei, who demolished the uprising headquarters the same day and set out to arrest the revolutionaries listed in the beadroll.[[55]](#cite_note-gongtong6-3-57) This forced the revolutionaries to launch the uprising earlier than planned.[[51]](#cite_note-tonsi86-53)

On the night of October 10, the revolutionaries fired shots to signal the uprising at the engineering barracks of Hubei [New Army](/wiki/New_Army "New Army").[[51]](#cite_note-tonsi86-53) They then led the New Army of all barracks to join the revolution.[[56]](#cite_note-spence-58) Under the guidance of Wu Zhaolin, Cai Jimin and others, this revolutionary army seized the official residence of the governor and government offices.[[54]](#cite_note-Wangke-56) Rui Cheng fled in panic into the Chuyu ship. Zhang Biao, the commander of the Qing army, also fled the city. On the morning of the 11th, the revolutionary army took the whole city of Wuchang, but leaders such as Jiang Yiwu and Sun Wu disappeared.[[51]](#cite_note-tonsi86-53) Thus the leaderless revolutionary army recommended [Li Yuanhong](/wiki/Li_Yuanhong "Li Yuanhong"), the assistant governor of the Qing army, as the commander-in-chief.[[57]](#cite_note-Harrison-59) Li founded the Hubei Military Government, proclaimed the abolition of the Qing rule in Hubei, the founding of the Republic of China and published an open telegram calling for other provinces to join the revolution.[[51]](#cite_note-tonsi86-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Wangke-56)

As the revolution spread to other parts of the country, the Qing government concentrated loyalist military forces to suppress the uprising in Wuhan. From October 17 to December 1, the revolutionary army and local volunteers defended the city in the [Battle of Yangxia](/wiki/Battle_of_Yangxia "Battle of Yangxia") against better armed and more numerous Qing forces commanded by [Yuan Shikai](/wiki/Yuan_Shikai "Yuan Shikai"). [Huang Xing](/wiki/Huang_Xing "Huang Xing") would arrive in Wuhan in early November to take command of the revolutionary army.[[54]](#cite_note-Wangke-56) After fierce fighting and heavy casualties, Qing forces seized Hankou and Hanyang. But Yuan agreed to halt the advance on Wuchang and participated in [peace talks](/wiki/Peace_talks "Peace talks"), which would eventually lead to the return of Sun Yat-sen from exile, founding of the Republic of China on January 1, 1912.[[53]](#cite_note-Welland,_Sasah_Su-ling_2007_pg_87-55)[[58]](#cite_note-Bergere-60) Through the Wuchang Uprising, Wuhan is known as the birthplace of the Xinhai Revolution, named after the Xinhai year on the Chinese calendar.[[59]](#cite_note-tvbs-61) The city has several museums and memorials to the revolution and the thousands of martyrs who died defending the revolution.

### Republic of China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Republic of China")]

See also: [Wuhan nationalist government](/wiki/Wuhan_nationalist_government "Wuhan nationalist government")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Hankou_1930.jpg/250px-Hankou_1930.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hankou_1930.jpg)

A map of Wuhan painted by the Japanese in 1930, with Hankou being the most prosperous sector

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Meeting_room_of_former_building_of_Wuhan_nationalist_government.jpg/250px-Meeting_room_of_former_building_of_Wuhan_nationalist_government.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meeting_room_of_former_building_of_Wuhan_nationalist_government.jpg)

Nanyang Building Interior

With the northern extension of the [Northern Expedition](/wiki/Northern_Expedition "Northern Expedition"), the center of the Great Revolution shifted from the Pearl River basin to the Yangtze River basin. On November 26, the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") Central Political Committee decided to [move the capital](/wiki/Historical_capitals_of_China "Historical capitals of China") from Guangzhou to Wuhan. In mid-December, most of the KMT central executive commissioners and national government commissioners arrived in Wuhan, set up the temporary joint conference of central executive commissioners and National Government commissioners, performed the top functions of central party headquarters and National Government, declared they would work in Wuhan on January 1, 1927, and decided to combine the towns of Wuchang, [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou "Hankou"), and Hanyang into Wuhan City, called "Capital District". The new national government, later known as "[Wuhan nationalist government](/wiki/Wuhan_nationalist_government "Wuhan nationalist government")", was based in the Nanyang Building in Hankou, while the central party headquarters and other organizations chose their locations in Hankou or Wuchang.[[17]](#cite_note-Remaking_the_Chinese_City-18)

In March 1927, [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") appeared at the Third Plenum of the KMT Central Executive Committee in Wuhan, which sought to strip General Chiang of his power by appointing [Wang Jingwei](/wiki/Wang_Jingwei "Wang Jingwei") leader. The first phase of the Northern Expedition was interrupted by the political split in the Kuomintang following the formation of the [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") faction in April 1927 against the existing faction in Wuhan.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor200968-62) Members of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"), who had survived the April 12 massacre, met at Wuhan and reelected [Chen Duxiu](/wiki/Chen_Duxiu "Chen Duxiu") (Ch'en Tu-hsiu) as the Party's Secretary General.[[61]](#cite_note-63) The split was partially motivated by the [purge of the Communists](/wiki/Shanghai_Massacre "Shanghai Massacre") within the party, which marked the end of the [First United Front](/wiki/First_United_Front "First United Front"), and [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek") briefly stepped down as the commander of the National Revolutionary Army.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor200972-64)

In June 1927, [Stalin](/wiki/Stalin "Stalin") sent a telegram to the Communists in Wuhan, calling for the mobilization of an army of workers and peasants.[[63]](#cite_note-65) This alarmed Wang Jingwei, who decided to break with the Communists and come to terms with Chiang Kai-shek. The [Wuhan coup](/wiki/Wuhan_coup "Wuhan coup") was a political shift made on July 15, 1927, by Wang Jingwei towards Chiang Kai-shek, and his [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai")-based rival in the Kuomintang. The Wuhan Nationalist Government was established in Wuhan on February 21, 1927, and ended by August 19, 1927.[[64]](#cite_note-Clark-66) After the end of the Northern Expedition, Hankou was elevated to a centrally-controlled municipality.

In the [1931 China floods](/wiki/1931_China_floods "1931 China floods"), one of the deadliest flood disasters in world history, Wuhan was a refuge for flood victims from outlying areas, who had been arriving since the late spring. But when the city itself was inundated in the early summer, and after a catastrophic dike failure just before 6:00 AM on July 27,[[65]](#cite_note-Graves-67): 270 an estimated 782,189 urban citizens and rural refugees were left homeless. The flood covered an area of 32 square miles and the city was flooded under many feet of water for close to three months.[[65]](#cite_note-Graves-67): 269–270 Large numbers gathered on flood islands throughout the city, with 30,000 sheltering on a railway embankment in central Hankou. With little food and a complete breakdown in sanitation, thousands soon began to succumb to diseases.[[66]](#cite_note-Courtney-68) Jin Shilong, Senior Engineer at the Hubei Flood Prevention Agency, described the flooding:

> There was no warning, only a sudden great wall of water. Most of Wuhan's buildings in those days were only one story high, and for many people there was no escape – they died by the tens of thousands. ... I was just coming off duty at the company's main office, a fairly new three-story building near the center of town ... When I heard the terrible noise and saw the wall of water coming, I raced to the top story of the building. ... I was in one of the tallest and strongest buildings left standing. At that time no one knew whether the water would subside or rise even higher.[[65]](#cite_note-Graves-67): 270

The high-water mark was reached on August 19 at Hankou, with the water level exceeding 16 m (53 ft) above normal.[[67]](#cite_note-pietz-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70) In 1936, when natural disaster struck [Central China](/wiki/Central_China "Central China") with widespread flooding affecting [Hebei](/wiki/Hebei "Hebei"), [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan "Hunan"), [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi"), Wuhan and [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing") caused by the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") and [Huai Rivers](/wiki/Huai_River "Huai River") bursting their banks, [Ong Seok Kim](/wiki/Ong_Seok_Kim "Ong Seok Kim"), as Chairman of the Sitiawan Fundraising and Disaster Relief Committee, raised money and materials in support of the victims.[[69]](#cite_note-eresources-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72)[[71]](#cite_note-73)[[72]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Zhongshan_Warship_1.jpg/250px-Zhongshan_Warship_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhongshan_Warship_1.jpg)

The gunboat *Zhongshan*

During the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") and following the [fall of Nanking](/wiki/Battle_of_Nanking "Battle of Nanking") in December 1937, Wuhan had become the provisional capital of China's Kuomintang government, and became another focal point of pitched air battles beginning in early 1938 between modern [monoplane](/wiki/Monoplane "Monoplane") bomber and fighter aircraft of the Imperial Japanese forces and the [Chinese Air Force](/wiki/Republic_of_China_Air_Force "Republic of China Air Force"), which included support from the [Soviet Volunteer Group](/wiki/Soviet_Volunteer_Group "Soviet Volunteer Group") in both planes and personnel, as U.S. support in war materials waned. As the battle raged on through 1938, Wuhan and the surrounding region had become the site of the [Battle of Wuhan](/wiki/Battle_of_Wuhan "Battle of Wuhan"). After being taken by the Japanese in late 1938, Wuhan became a major Japanese logistics center for operations in southern China.
In early October 1938, Japanese troops moved east and north in the outskirts of Wuhan. As a result, numerous companies and enterprises and large numbers of people had to withdraw from Wuhan to the west of Hubei and Sichuan. The KMT navy undertook the responsibility of defending the Yangtze River on patrol and covering the withdrawal. On October 24, while overseeing the waters of the Yangtze River near the town of Jinkou (Jiangxia District in Wuhan) in Wuchang, the KMT [gunboat](/wiki/Gunboat "Gunboat") *[Zhongshan](/wiki/Chinese_gunboat_Chung_Shan "Chinese gunboat Chung Shan")* came up against six Japanese aircraft. Though two were eventually shot down, the *Zhongshan* sank with 25 casualties. Raised from the bottom of the Yangtze River in 1997, and restored at a local shipyard, the *Zhongshan* has been moved to a purpose-built museum in Wuhan's suburban [Jiangxia District](/wiki/Jiangxia_District "Jiangxia District"), which opened on September 26, 2011.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a key center on the Yangtze, Wuhan was an important base for Japanese operations in China.[[73]](#cite_note-75) On December 18, 1944, in a planned strategic move, and as revenge for the torture and execution of three captured American pilots by Japanese soldiers in the city, Wuhan was bombed by 77 American bombers with the approval of Chiang Kai-Shek. This set off a firestorm that destroyed much of the military resources of the city.[[74]](#cite_note-Fenby,_Jonathan_page_447-76) For the next three days, Wuhan was bombed by the Americans, destroying all of the docks and warehouses of Wuhan, as well as the Japanese air bases in the city. The air raids also killed thousands of Chinese civilians.[[74]](#cite_note-Fenby,_Jonathan_page_447-76) "According to casualty statistics compiled by Hankou city in 1946, more than 20,000 were killed or injured in the December bombings of 1944."[[75]](#cite_note-77)

Wuhan returned to Chinese control in September 1945. Administratively, Wuchang and Hanyang were initially combined into a new City of Wuchang, but in October 1946 were separated into the City of Wuchang (including Wuchang only) and the County of Hanyang. Hankou became a centrally controlled municipality in August 1947. Militarily, the Wuhan Forward Headquarters was established in Wuhan, headed by [Bai Chongxi](/wiki/Bai_Chongxi "Bai Chongxi").[[76]](#cite_note-78)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/PLA_troops_enter_to_Hankou.jpg/250px-PLA_troops_enter_to_Hankou.jpg)](/wiki/File:PLA_troops_enter_to_Hankou.jpg)

[People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") troops at Zhongshan Avenue, Hankou on May 16, 1949

During the later stages of the Chinese Civil War, Bai sought to broker peace, proposing that the Communist Party could rule northern China while the Nationalist government retained southern China. This was rejected, and on May 15, 1949, Bai and the Wuhan garrison retreated from the city. People's Liberation Army troops entered Wuhan on the afternoon of Monday, May 16, 1949.[[77]](#cite_note-79)[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81)

### People's Republic of China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: People's Republic of China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Wuhan-Flood-Memorial-0220.jpg/250px-Wuhan-Flood-Memorial-0220.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wuhan-Flood-Memorial-0220.jpg)

In his poem "Swimming" (1956), engraved on the [1954 Flood](/wiki/1954_Yangtze_River_Floods "1954 Yangtze River Floods") Memorial in Wuhan, [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") envisions "walls of stone" to be erected upstream.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

The Communists redeveloped industry in Wuhan, which had been damaged by war.[[81]](#cite_note-:Chatwin-83): 48 During the PRC's first decade, it became an important center of industry again.[[81]](#cite_note-:Chatwin-83): 48 Hundreds of factories were built in the city, including most prominently [Wuhan Iron and Steel](/wiki/Wuhan_Iron_and_Steel_Corporation "Wuhan Iron and Steel Corporation"), which opened in 1958.[[81]](#cite_note-:Chatwin-83): 48

The [Changjiang Water Resources Commission](/wiki/Changjiang_Water_Resources_Commission "Changjiang Water Resources Commission") was reestablished in February 1950 with its headquarters in Wuhan. From June to September 1954, the [Yangtze River Floods](/wiki/1954_Yangtze_River_floods "1954 Yangtze River floods") were a series of catastrophic floodings that occurred mostly in Hubei Province. Due to an unusually high volume of precipitation as well as an extraordinarily long rainy season in the middle stretch of the Yangtze River late in the spring of 1954, the river started to rise above its usual level in around late June. In 1969, a large stone monument was erected in the riverside park in Hankou honoring the heroic deeds in fighting the 1954 Yangtze River floods.

Before construction of the [Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge"), [Hunslet Engine Company](/wiki/Hunslet_Engine_Company "Hunslet Engine Company") built two extra heavy [0-8-0](/wiki/0-8-0 "0-8-0") locomotives for loading the [train ferries](/wiki/Train_ferry "Train ferry") for crossing the Yangtze River in Wuhan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge_in_construction.jpg/250px-Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge_in_construction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge_in_construction.jpg)

Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge during Construction

The project of building the [Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge"), also known as the First Yangtze River Bridge, was regarded as one of the key projects during the first five-year plan. On October 25, 1955, construction began on the bridge proper. The same day in 1957, the whole project was completed and an opening-to-traffic ceremony was held on October 15. The First Yangtze River Bridge united the [Beijing–Hankou railway](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Hankou_railway "Beijing–Hankou railway") with the [Guangdong–Hankou railway](/wiki/Guangzhou%E2%80%93Hankou_railway "Guangzhou–Hankou railway") into the [Beijing–Guangzhou railway](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Guangzhou_railway "Beijing–Guangzhou railway"), making Wuhan a 'thoroughfare to nine provinces' ([九省通衢](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B9%9D%E7%9C%81%E9%80%9A%E8%A1%A2 "wikt:九省通衢")) in name and in fact.

After Chengdu Conference, Mao went to Chongqing and Wuhan in April to inspect the countryside and factories. In Wuhan, he called all the leaders of provinces and municipalities who had not attended Chengdu Conference to report their work. Tian Jiaying, the secretary of Mao, said that Wuhan Conference was a supplement to Chengdu Conference.[[82]](#cite_note-:9-84)

As the [Third Front campaign](/wiki/Third_Front_(China) "Third Front (China)") shifted the focus of industrial development to China's hinterlands, Wuhan's development slowed.[[81]](#cite_note-:Chatwin-83): 48

In July 1967, civil strife struck the city in the [Wuhan Incident](/wiki/Wuhan_Incident "Wuhan Incident") ("July 20th Incident"), an armed conflict between two hostile groups who were fighting for control over the city at the height of the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution").[[83]](#cite_note-85) Economic development was further disrupted by the Cultural Revolution.[[81]](#cite_note-:Chatwin-83): 48

In 1981, the Wuhan City Government commenced reconstruction of the [Yellow Crane Tower](/wiki/Yellow_Crane_Tower "Yellow Crane Tower") at a new location, about 1 km (0.62 mi) from the original site, and it was completed in 1985. In 1957, the [Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge") was built with one trestle of the bridge on the site of the tower, which had been last destroyed in 1884.[[84]](#cite_note-Wang2016-86)

During the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests "1989 Tiananmen Square protests"), students in Wuhan blocked the [Yangtze River Railway bridge](/wiki/Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge") and another 4,000 gathered at the railway station.[[85]](#cite_note-Zhang2001-87): 400 About one thousand students staged a railroad 'sit-in'. Rail traffic on the Beijing-Guangzhou and Wuhan-Dalian lines was interrupted. The students also urged employees of major state-owned enterprises to go on strike.[[85]](#cite_note-Zhang2001-87): 405 The situation was so tense that residents reportedly began a [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") and resorted to panic-buying.[[85]](#cite_note-Zhang2001-87): 408

Wuhan was the first stop on [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping")'s 1992 [southern tour](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping%27s_southern_tour "Deng Xiaoping's southern tour").[[81]](#cite_note-:Chatwin-83): 48

In the wake of the [United States bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade](/wiki/United_States_bombing_of_the_Chinese_embassy_in_Belgrade "United States bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade") on May 7, 1999, protests broke out throughout China, including in Wuhan.[[86]](#cite_note-washpost-88)

On June 22, 2000, a [Wuhan Airlines flight](/wiki/Wuhan_Airlines_Flight_343 "Wuhan Airlines Flight 343") from [Enshi](/wiki/Enshi_Xujiaping_Airport "Enshi Xujiaping Airport") to Wuhan was forced to circle for 30 minutes due to thunderstorms. The aircraft eventually crashed on the banks of [Han River](/wiki/Han_River_(Hanshui) "Han River (Hanshui)") in [Hanyang District](/wiki/Hanyang_District "Hanyang District"),[[87]](#cite_note-89) all on-board perished (there were varying accounts of number of crews and passengers). In addition, the crash also killed 7 people on the ground.[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-92)

[Chinese protesters organized boycotts](/wiki/China%E2%80%93France_relations#2008_rifts "China–France relations") of the French-owned retail chain [Carrefour](/wiki/Carrefour "Carrefour") in major Chinese cities including [Kunming](/wiki/Kunming "Kunming"), [Hefei](/wiki/Hefei "Hefei") and Wuhan, accusing the French nation of pro-[secessionist](/wiki/Secession "Secession") conspiracy and [anti-Chinese](/wiki/Anti-Chinese "Anti-Chinese") racism.[[91]](#cite_note-reuters1-93) The BBC reported that hundreds of people demonstrated in Beijing, Wuhan, Hefei, Kunming and [Qingdao](/wiki/Qingdao "Qingdao").[[92]](#cite_note-news1-94)[[91]](#cite_note-reuters1-93) On May 19, 2011, [Fang Binxing](/wiki/Fang_Binxing "Fang Binxing"), the Principal of [Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications](/wiki/Beijing_University_of_Posts_and_Telecommunications "Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications") (also known as "Father of [China's Great Fire Wall](/wiki/Great_Firewall_of_China "Great Firewall of China")"[[93]](#cite_note-95)) was hit on the chest by a shoe thrown at him by a [Huazhong University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Huazhong_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Huazhong University of Science and Technology") student who calls herself "hanjunyi" (寒君依, or 小湖北) while Fang was giving a lecture at [Wuhan University](/wiki/Wuhan_University "Wuhan University").[[94]](#cite_note-96)[[95]](#cite_note-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99)[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101)

Jiangtan street along [Yangze river](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze")

The city has been subject to devastating floods, which are now intended to be controlled by the ambitious [Three Gorges Dam](/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam "Three Gorges Dam"), a project which was completed in 2008.[[100]](#cite_note-102)[[101]](#cite_note-103) The [2008 Chinese winter storms](/wiki/2008_Chinese_winter_storms "2008 Chinese winter storms") damaged water supply equipment in Wuhan: up to 100,000 people were out of running water when several water pipes burst, cutting the supply to local households.[[102]](#cite_note-reuteralertnet-104) The [2010 Northern Hemisphere summer heat wave](/wiki/2010_Northern_Hemisphere_summer_heat_waves "2010 Northern Hemisphere summer heat waves") hit Wuhan on July 3.[[103]](#cite_note-english.sina.com-105)
In the [2010 China floods](/wiki/2010_China_floods "2010 China floods"), the [Han River](/wiki/Han_River_(Hanshui) "Han River (Hanshui)") at Wuhan experienced its worst flooding in twenty years, as officials continued sandbagging efforts along the Han and Yangtze Rivers in the city and checked reservoirs.[[104]](#cite_note-guardian28-106) In the [2011 China floods](/wiki/2011_China_floods "2011 China floods"), Wuhan was flooded, with parts of the city losing power.[[105]](#cite_note-107) In the [2016 China floods](/wiki/2016_China_floods "2016 China floods"), Wuhan saw 570 mm (22 in) of rainfall during the first week of July, surpassing the record that fell on the city in 1991. A [red alert](/wiki/Flood_alert "Flood alert") for heavy rainfall was issued on July 2, the same day that eight people died after a 15-meter (49 ft) section of a 2 m (6.6 ft) tall wall collapsed on top of them.[[106]](#cite_note-108) The city's subway system, the [Wuhan Metro](/wiki/Wuhan_Metro "Wuhan Metro") was partially submerged as was the [main railway station](/wiki/Wuhan_Railway_Station "Wuhan Railway Station").[[107]](#cite_note-scmp-109) At least 14 city residents were killed, one was missing, and more than 80,000 were relocated.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

The [2019 Military World Games](/wiki/2019_Military_World_Games "2019 Military World Games") were hosted in Wuhan in October.[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[110]](#cite_note-112)

In December 2019, [SARS-CoV-2](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome_coronavirus_2 "Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2"), a novel coronavirus that caused the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), was first discovered in Wuhan,[[20]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times-21)[[21]](#cite_note-Oxford_University-22) and the city was the location of the first [lockdown](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdowns "COVID-19 lockdowns") of the pandemic in January 2020.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-23) [Wuhan and other Hubei cities were placed under lockdown](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdown_in_China "COVID-19 lockdown in China") for nearly three months to contain the disease.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[111]](#cite_note-113) On April 8, 2020, the Wuhan lockdown officially came to an end after no new domestic cases were reported in Hubei province.[[112]](#cite_note-114) SARS-CoV-2 is believed to have resulted from the [zoonotic spillover](/wiki/Spillover_infection "Spillover infection") of a virus that existed in bats,[[113]](#cite_note-University_of_Oxford_2021-115) and the [Huanan Seafood Market](/wiki/Huanan_Seafood_Wholesale_Market "Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market") in Wuhan has been identified as a potential site for the initial animal-to-human transmission.[[114]](#cite_note-116) Although no bats were sold at the market, some 38 other species of animals were offered at wet markets in the city, one of which could have served as an intermediary species.[[113]](#cite_note-University_of_Oxford_2021-115)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Txu-oclc-10552568-nh50-5.jpg/250px-Txu-oclc-10552568-nh50-5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Txu-oclc-10552568-nh50-5.jpg)

Map including the Wuhan area ([AMS](/wiki/Army_Map_Service "Army Map Service"), 1953)

Wuhan is in east-central Hubei, at latitude 29° 58'–31° 22' N and longitude 113° 41'–115° 05' E. Wuhan sits at the confluence of the [Han River](/wiki/Han_River_(Hubei) "Han River (Hubei)") flowing into the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") at the East of the Jianghan Plain along the Yangtze's middle reaches.

The metropolitan area comprises three parts—[Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang,_Hubei "Wuchang, Hubei"), [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou_District "Hankou District"), and [Hanyang](/wiki/Hanyang_District "Hanyang District")—commonly called the "Three Towns of Wuhan" (hence the name "Wuhan", combining "Wu" from the first city and "Han" from the other two). The consolidation of these cities occurred in 1927 and Wuhan was thereby established. The three former cities face each other across the rivers and are linked by bridges, including one of the first modern bridges in China, known as the "First Bridge".

* Wuchang lies south east of the Yangtze River that separates it from both Hankou and Hanyang.
* Hankou sits north of the Yangtze River separating it from Wuchang. Hankou is north of the Han River separating it from Hanyang.
* Hanyang lies west of the Yangtze separating it from Wuchang. Hanyang is south of the Han river separating it from Hankou.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Mount_Mo_and_East_Lake%2C_Wuhan.jpg/250px-Mount_Mo_and_East_Lake%2C_Wuhan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_Mo_and_East_Lake,_Wuhan.jpg)

[East Lake](/wiki/East_Lake_(Wuhan) "East Lake (Wuhan)")

It is simple in terrain—low and flat in the middle and hilly in the south, with the Yangtze and Han rivers winding through the city. The [She River](/wiki/She_River "She River") enters the Yangtze in Huangpi District. Wuhan occupies a land area of 8,494.41 square kilometers (3,279.71 sq mi), most of which is alluvial plain and decorated with hills and a great number of lakes and ponds. Water makes up one quarter of Wuhan's urban territory, which is the highest percentage among major cities in China.[[115]](#cite_note-nl-117) Wuhan has nearly 200 lakes, including the [East Lake](/wiki/East_Lake_(Wuhan) "East Lake (Wuhan)") of 33 km2, and [Tangxun Lake](/wiki/Tangxun_Lake "Tangxun Lake"), which are the largest lakes entirely within a city in China.[[115]](#cite_note-nl-117)

Other well-known lakes include [South Lake](/wiki/South_Lake_(Wuhan) "South Lake (Wuhan)") and [Sand Lake](/w/index.php?title=Sand_Lake_(Wuhan)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sand Lake (Wuhan) (page does not exist)"). [Liangzi Lake](/wiki/Liangzi_Lake "Liangzi Lake"), the largest lake by surface area in Hubei province, is located in the southeast of [Jiangxia District](/wiki/Jiangxia_District "Jiangxia District").The twin peaks at the junction of Huangpi District and Xiaogan City, with an altitude of 872.5 meters, are the highest points in Wuhan. There are also several mountains within the city limits of Wuhan including [Mount Luojia](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Luojia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mount Luojia (page does not exist)") (珞珈山) in Wuchang District as well as [Mount Hong](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Hong&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mount Hong (page does not exist)") (洪山) and [Mount Yujia](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Yujia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mount Yujia (page does not exist)") (喻家山/瑜珈山) in [Hongshan District](/wiki/Hongshan_District,_Wuhan "Hongshan District, Wuhan").[[116]](#cite_note-118)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Wuhan's climate is [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical "Humid subtropical") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfa*) with abundant rainfall in summer and four distinctive seasons. Wuhan is known for its humid summers, when [dewpoints](/wiki/Dewpoint "Dewpoint") can often reach 26 °C (79 °F) or more.[[117]](#cite_note-119) Historically, along with [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing") and [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), Wuhan is referred to as one of the "[Three Furnacelike Cities](/wiki/Three_Furnaces "Three Furnaces")" along the Yangtze River for their hot summers.[[118]](#cite_note-120) However, the climate data of recent years suggests that Wuhan is no longer among the top tier of "The hottest cities in summer" list, the New Four Furnacelike Cities are [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou "Fuzhou"), [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), and [Nanchang](/wiki/Nanchang "Nanchang").[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122) Spring and autumn are generally mild, while winter is cool with quite low rainfall and occasional snow. The monthly 24-hour average temperature ranges from 4.1 °C (39.4 °F) in January to 29.3 °C (84.7 °F) in July.[[121]](#cite_note-cma_graphical-123) Annual precipitation totals just under 1,320 mm (52 in),[[121]](#cite_note-cma_graphical-123) the majority of which falls from April to July. The annual mean temperature is 17.4 °C (63.3 °F),[[121]](#cite_note-cma_graphical-123) while the frost-free period lasts 211 to 272 days.[[122]](#cite_note-124) With monthly possible sunshine percentage ranging from 30 percent in January to 53 percent in August, the city proper receives 1,783 hours of bright sunshine annually.[[123]](#cite_note-CMA_old-125) Extreme low and high temperatures recorded are −18.1 °C (−1 °F) on January 31, 1977, and 39.7 °C (103 °F) on July 27, 2017 / on August 18, 2022 (unofficial record of 41.3 °C (106 °F) on August 10, 1934).[[124]](#cite_note-126)[[125]](#cite_note-127)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Wuhan is a [sub-provincial city](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city"). Municipal government is regulated by the local [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP), led by the Wuhan [Party Secretary](/wiki/Party_Secretary_of_Wuhan "Party Secretary of Wuhan") ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 武汉市委书记), Wang Zhonglin (王忠林). The local CCP issues administrative orders, collects taxes, manages the economy, and directs a standing committee of the Municipal People's Congress in making policy decisions and overseeing the local government.

Government officials include the Mayor of Wuhan (市长), Cheng Yongwen ([程用文](/w/index.php?title=%E7%A8%8B%E7%94%A8%E6%96%87&action=edit&redlink=1 "程用文 (page does not exist)")[[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%A8%8B%E7%94%A8%E6%96%87 "zh:程用文")]), and vice-mayors. Numerous bureaus focus on law, public security, and other affairs. [Zhou Xianwang](/wiki/Zhou_Xianwang "Zhou Xianwang") (周先旺) was mayor from 2018 to 2021.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy")]

Up until the 21st century, Wuhan was largely an agricultural region. Since 2004 it has been a focal point of the [Rise of Central China Plan](/wiki/Rise_of_Central_China_Plan "Rise of Central China Plan"), which aims to build less-developed inland economies into hubs of advanced manufacturing.

Since 1890,[[115]](#cite_note-nl-117) the steel industry has been the backbone of Wuhan's industry.[[139]](#cite_note-hubeigov-141) In 2010, automobile industry exceeded GDP for Wuhan Iron and Steel Corporation (WISCO) steel for the first time. There are 5 car manufacturers, including [Dongfeng Honda](/wiki/Dongfeng_Honda "Dongfeng Honda"), [Citroën](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn "Citroën"), [SAIC-GM](/wiki/SAIC-GM "SAIC-GM"), DFM Passenger Vehicle and [Dongfeng Renault](/wiki/Dongfeng_Renault "Dongfeng Renault"). [Dongfeng-Citroen Automobile Co., Ltd](/wiki/Dongfeng_Peugeot-Citro%C3%ABn "Dongfeng Peugeot-Citroën") is headquartered in the city.[[139]](#cite_note-hubeigov-141)

As of 2016, Wuhan has attracted foreign investment from over 80 countries, with 5,973 foreign-invested enterprises established in the city with a total capital injection of $22.45 billion [USD](/wiki/USD "USD").[[140]](#cite_note-china-briefing.com-142) Among these, about 50 French companies including [Renault](/wiki/Renault "Renault") and [PSA Group](/wiki/PSA_Group "PSA Group") have operations in the city, representing over one third of French investment in China, and the highest level of French investment in any Chinese city.[[141]](#cite_note-143)

Wuhan is an important center for economy, trade, finance, transportation, information technology, and education in China. Its major industries include optic-electronic, automobile manufacturing, iron and steel manufacturing, new pharmaceutical sector, biology engineering, new materials industry and environmental protection. Environmental sustainability is highlighted in Wuhan's list of emerging industries, which include energy efficiency technology and renewable energy.[[140]](#cite_note-china-briefing.com-142)

As of 2021, Wuhan is ranked among the world's top 100 financial centers, according to the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[32]](#cite_note-GFCI-33)

### Industrial zones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Industrial zones")]

Major industrial zones in Wuhan include in chronological order:

* Wuhan Economic and Technological Development Zone

Wuhan Economic and Technological Development Zone is a national level industrial zone incorporated in 1993.[[142]](#cite_note-rightsite1-144) Its current zone size is about 10–25 square km and it plans to expand to 25–50 square km. Industries encouraged in Wuhan Economic and Technological Development Zone include Auto-mobile Production/Assembly, Biotechnology/Pharmaceuticals, Chemicals Production and Processing, Food/Beverage Processing, Heavy Industry, and Telecommunications Equipment.

* Wuhan Export Processing Zone

Wuhan Export Processing Zone was established in 2000. It is located in Wuhan Economic and Technology Development Zone, planned to cover 2.7 square kilometers (1.0 square mile) of land. The first 0.7-square-kilometer (0.3-square-mile) area has already been created.[[143]](#cite_note-145)

* Wuhan [Donghu New Technology Development Zone](/wiki/Donghu_New_Technology_Development_Zone "Donghu New Technology Development Zone")

Wuhan Donghu New Technology Development Zone is a national level high-tech development zone. Optical-electronics, telecommunications, and equipment manufacturing are the core industries of Wuhan East Lake High-Tech Development Zone (ELHTZ) while software outsourcing and electronics are also encouraged. ELHTZ is China's largest production center for optoelectronic products with key players like Yangtze Optical Fiber and Cable,[[144]](#cite_note-146) (the largest fiber-optical cable maker in China), and Fiberhome Telecommunications.[[145]](#cite_note-147) Wuhan Donghu New Technology Development Zone also represents the development center for China's laser industry with key players such as [HGTECH](/wiki/HGTECH "HGTECH")[[146]](#cite_note-148) and Chutian Laser being based in the zone.[[147]](#cite_note-149)

* Wuhan Optical Valley (Guanggu) Software Park

Wuhan Optical Valley (Guanggu) Software Park is located in Wuhan [Donghu New Technology Development Zone](/wiki/Donghu_New_Technology_Development_Zone "Donghu New Technology Development Zone"). Wuhan Optics Valley Software Park is jointly developed by East Lake High-Tech Development Zone and Dalian Software Park Co., Ltd.[[148]](#cite_note-RightSite.asia-150) The planned area is 0.67 square kilometers (0.26 square miles) with total floor area of 6,000,000 square meters (65,000,000 square feet). The zone is 8.5 km (5.28 mi) away from the 316 National Highway and is 46.7 km (29.02 mi) away from the Wuhan Tianhe Airport.

* Wuhan [Biolake](/wiki/Biolake "Biolake")

Biolake is an industrial base established in 2008 in the [Optics Valley of China](/wiki/Optics_Valley_of_China "Optics Valley of China"). Located in [East Lake New Technology Development Zone](/wiki/Donghu_New_Technology_Development_Zone "Donghu New Technology Development Zone") of Wuhan, Biolake covers 15 km2 (5.8 sq mi), and has six parks including Bio-innovation Park, Bio-pharma Park, Bio-agriculture Park, Bio-manufacturing Park, Medical Device Park and Medical Health Park, to accommodate both research activities and living.[[149]](#cite_note-Gwinnett_Chamber_Economic_Development_signed_an_MOU_with_the_Wuhan_(China)_National_Bio-industry_Base_(Biolake)-151)[[150]](#cite_note-Profile_of_Biolake-152)[[151]](#cite_note-Medicilon_and_Wuhan_Biolake_successfully_organized_a_bio-pharmaceutical_salon-153)[[152]](#cite_note-CLSC_Partners_with_Wuhan_Biolake_for_China_Office-154)[[153]](#cite_note-Biolake_and_the_Booming_Bio-industry_in_Central_China-155)

Wuhan is the most populous city in Central China and [among the most populous in China](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_China_by_population_and_built-up_area "List of cities in China by population and built-up area"). In the [Seventh Census of China in 2020](/wiki/2020_Chinese_census "2020 Chinese census"), Wuhan was home to 12,326,500 inhabitants, a 25.97% increase by 2.5411 million compared to the last census in 2010. 2010-2020 is the fastest growing 10 years in history since the census was established, averaging 2.34% annually, and it was the first time that Wuhan's population reached 10 million.[[161]](#cite_note-163)

The encompassing [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") was estimated by the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") (Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development) to have, as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit), a population of 19 million.[[162]](#cite_note-164)[[163]](#cite_note-oecd2015-165)
As of November 2019, urban development status considering both spatial and socioeconomic processes has been examined using Night Time Lighting data and land cover data as proxies; it showed Wuhan's high concentration
of socioeconomic activities compared to its urban spatial development.[[164]](#cite_note-sensing-166)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religion in Wuhan (2017)[[165]](#cite_note-Han2017-167)

1. [Chinese religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") or [not religious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") (including [Taoists](/wiki/Taoists "Taoists") (0.9%)) (79.2%)
2. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (14.7%)
3. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (2.90%)
4. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (1.60%)
5. [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (0.30%)
6. Other (1.60%)

According to a survey published in 2017, 79.2% of the population of Wuhan are either irreligious or practice [worship of gods and ancestors](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"); among these 0.9% are [Taoists](/wiki/Taoists "Taoists"). Among other religious doctrines, 14.7% of the population adheres to [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), 2.9% to [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), 0.3% to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and 1.6% to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), and 1.6% of the population adheres to unspecified other religions.[[165]](#cite_note-Han2017-167)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Railways")]

[China Railway Wuhan Group](/wiki/China_Railway_Wuhan_Group "China Railway Wuhan Group") manages the [Wuhan Railway Hub](/wiki/Wuhan_Railway_Hub "Wuhan Railway Hub"). Wuhan Railway Hub is considered one of the four key [railway hubs](/wiki/Railway_hub "Railway hub") of China.[[166]](#cite_note-168) The city of Wuhan is served by three major railway stations: the [Hankou railway station](/wiki/Hankou_railway_station "Hankou railway station") in [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou_District "Hankou District"), the [Wuchang railway station](/wiki/Wuchang_railway_station "Wuchang railway station") in [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang,_Hubei "Wuchang, Hubei"), and the [Wuhan railway station](/wiki/Wuhan_railway_station "Wuhan railway station"), located in a newly developed area east of the [East Lake](/wiki/East_Lake_(Wuhan) "East Lake (Wuhan)") ([Hongshan District](/wiki/Hongshan_District,_Wuhan "Hongshan District, Wuhan")). As the stations are many miles apart, it is important for passengers to be aware of the particular station(s) used by a particular train.

The (original) Hankou Station was the terminus for the [Jinghan railway](/wiki/Jinghan_railway "Jinghan railway") from Beijing, while the Wuchang Station was the terminus for the [Yuehan railway](/wiki/Yuehan_railway "Yuehan railway") to Guangzhou. Since the construction of the First Yangtze Bridge and the linking of the two lines into the [Jingguang railway](/wiki/Jingguang_railway "Jingguang railway"), both Hankou and Wuchang stations have been served by trains going to all directions, which contrasts with the situation in such cities as New York or Moscow, where different stations serve different directions.

With the opening of the [Hefei-Wuhan high-speed railway](/wiki/Hewu_passenger_railway "Hewu passenger railway") on April 1, 2009,[[167]](#cite_note-169) Wuhan became served by high-speed trains with Hefei, Nanjing, and Shanghai; several trains a day now connect the city with Shanghai, getting there in under six hours. As of early 2010, most of these express trains leave from the [Hankou railway station](/wiki/Hankou_railway_station "Hankou railway station").

In 2006, construction began on the new [Wuhan railway station](/wiki/Wuhan_railway_station "Wuhan railway station") with 11 platforms, located on the northeastern outskirts of the city. In December 2009, the station was opened, as China unveiled its [second high-speed train](/wiki/Wuhan%E2%80%93Guangzhou_High-Speed_Railway "Wuhan–Guangzhou High-Speed Railway") with scheduled runs from Guangzhou to Wuhan. Billed as the fastest train in the world, it can reach a speed of 394 km/h (244.82 mph). The travel time between the two cities has been reduced from ten and a half hours to just three. The rail service has been extended north to Beijing.[[168]](#cite_note-170)

As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit), the new [Wuhan railway station](/wiki/Wuhan_railway_station "Wuhan railway station") is primarily used by the Wuhan-Guangzhou high-speed trains, while most regular trains to other destinations continue to use the Hankou and Wuchang stations.

Construction work is carried out on several lines of the new [Wuhan Metropolitan Area intercity railway](/wiki/Wuhan_Metropolitan_Area_intercity_railway "Wuhan Metropolitan Area intercity railway"), which will eventually connect Wuhan's three main rail terminals with several stations throughout the city's outer areas and farther suburbs, as well as with the nearby cities of [Xianning](/wiki/Xianning "Xianning"), [Huangshi](/wiki/Huangshi "Huangshi"), [Huanggang](/wiki/Huanggang "Huanggang"), and [Xiaogan](/wiki/Xiaogan "Xiaogan"). The first line of the system, [the one to Xianning](/wiki/Wuhan%E2%80%93Xianning_Intercity_Railway "Wuhan–Xianning Intercity Railway"), opened for passenger operations at the end of 2013. The line to [Xiaogen](/wiki/Wuhan%E2%80%93Xiaogan_intercity_railway "Wuhan–Xiaogan intercity railway") opened on December 1, 2016, and it was extended with the opening of the [Wuhan–Shiyan high-speed railway](/wiki/Wuhan%E2%80%93Shiyan_high-speed_railway "Wuhan–Shiyan high-speed railway") to Shiyan on November 29, 2019.[[169]](#cite_note-171)[[170]](#cite_note-172)

The main freight railway station and [classification yard](/wiki/Classification_yard "Classification yard") of the Wuhan metropolitan area is the extensive [Wuhan North railway station](/wiki/Wuhan_North_railway_station "Wuhan North railway station"), with 112 tracks and over 650 switches. It is located in [Hengdian Subdistrict](/w/index.php?title=Hengdian_Subdistrict&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hengdian Subdistrict (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%A8%AA%E5%BA%97%E8%A1%97%E9%81%93 "zh:横店街道")] of [Huangpi District](/wiki/Huangpi_District "Huangpi District"), located 20 km (12 mi) north of the Wuhan Station and 23 km (14 mi) from Hankou Station.

### Metro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Metro")]

Main article: [Wuhan Metro](/wiki/Wuhan_Metro "Wuhan Metro")

[Wuhan Metro](/wiki/Wuhan_Metro "Wuhan Metro") is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system serving the city of Wuhan. Owned and operated by Wuhan Metro Group Co., Ltd., the network now includes 11 lines, 282 stations, and 435 km (270 mi) of route length. Line 1, the first line in the system, opened on July 28, 2004, making Wuhan the seventh city in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") with a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system, after [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing_Subway "Beijing Subway"), [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin_Metro "Tianjin Metro"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro "Shanghai Metro"), [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou_Metro "Guangzhou Metro"), [Changchun](/wiki/Changchun_Rail_Transit "Changchun Rail Transit"), and [Dalian](/wiki/Dalian_Metro "Dalian Metro").[[171]](#cite_note-urbanrail1-173) Line 2 opened on December 28, 2012, and is the first underground metro line crossing the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"). Commuting across the Yangtze River and Han River has been the bottleneck of Wuhan traffic. However, the appearance of Wuhan Metro greatly relieved this problem. With 1.22 billion annual passengers in 2019, Wuhan Metro is the sixth-busiest rapid transit system in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China").[[172]](#cite_note-全来了！2019年中国城市地铁客运量总结-174) Wuhan Metro is a rapidly developing metro system. There are a number of lines or sections under construction. The government of Wuhan City promised the citizens that at least two lines or sections open every year.[[173]](#cite_note-175) Due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the entire network was out of service from January 23 to March 27, 2020.
