[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Optics_Valley_Suspended_Monorail_%282%29.jpg/250px-Optics_Valley_Suspended_Monorail_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Optics_Valley_Suspended_Monorail_(2).jpg)

Optics Valley Sky Rail

Main article: [Trams in Wuhan](/wiki/Trams_in_Wuhan "Trams in Wuhan")

Trams were brought to the streets of Wuhan on July 28, 2017, with the first line (Auto-city T1 Line) opened that day.[[174]](#cite_note-xinhuanet-T1-176) The trams under construction or planning in Wuhan are:

* Auto-city trams, with Lines T1, T2, T6, and T8 in the Wuhan Economic Development Area, in the far western reaches on Hanyang. T1 Line is operational as of 2017.
* Optics Valley trams, two lines (T1 and T2) south and east of Guanggu Circle (*Guanggu Guangchang*) in southeastern Wuchang. The system opened on January 18, 2018.[[175]](#cite_note-177)
* The Old Hankou Streetcar, a loop line around Hankou city.

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Buses")]

Local transport is also provided by buses, including [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus"). The trolleybus system has been in operation since 1958.[[176]](#cite_note-murray-178) Its first route, which remains in operation today, is [route 1](/wiki/Wuhan_trolleybus_route_1 "Wuhan trolleybus route 1").

### Maritime transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Maritime transport")]

Wuhan is a major hub for maritime transport in central China. The [Port of Wuhan](/wiki/Port_of_Wuhan "Port of Wuhan") provides services for the local population and shipping services.

### Ferry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ferry")]

Located on the banks of the Yangtze River, Wuhan has a long history of ferry services. Modern ferry services were established in 1900 by steam boat. In 1937, a train ferry was established to transport train cars from Hankou to Wuchang.[[177]](#cite_note-火车轮渡守候47年的人文景观-179) There are numbered stops around Wuhan where people can get on and off the ferry and there is a tourist ferry in the night.

Currently, ferry services are provided by the Wuhan Ferry Company. In 2010, the company bought ten new ships to replace those that had been in service for 29 years.[[178]](#cite_note-Wuhan_Ferry-180)

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Airports")]

Main article: [Wuhan Tianhe International Airport](/wiki/Wuhan_Tianhe_International_Airport "Wuhan Tianhe International Airport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Tianhe_Airport_Terminal_3_%2803%29.jpg/250px-Tianhe_Airport_Terminal_3_%2803%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tianhe_Airport_Terminal_3_(03).jpg)

Terminal 3 of Wuhan Tianhe Airport

[Wuhan Tianhe International Airport](/wiki/Wuhan_Tianhe_International_Airport "Wuhan Tianhe International Airport") is one of the busiest airports in central China. The airport opened in April 1995 to replace the old Hankou Wangjiadun Airport and [Nanhu Airport](/wiki/Wuhan_Nanhu_Airport "Wuhan Nanhu Airport") as the major airport of Wuhan.[[179]](#cite_note-181)[[180]](#cite_note-182) It is located in Wuhan's suburban [Huangpi District](/wiki/Huangpi_District "Huangpi District"), 26 kilometers (16 mi) north of Wuhan city proper. The extension of Line 2 of [Wuhan Metro](/wiki/Wuhan_Metro "Wuhan Metro") to Tianhe Airport opened on December 28, 2016.[[181]](#cite_note-183) It has also been selected as China's fourth international hub airport after [Beijing Capital](/wiki/Beijing_Capital_International_Airport "Beijing Capital International Airport"), [Shanghai Pudong](/wiki/Shanghai_Pudong_International_Airport "Shanghai Pudong International Airport") and [Guangzhou Baiyun](/wiki/Guangzhou_Baiyun_International_Airport "Guangzhou Baiyun International Airport"). A second terminal was completed in March 2008, having been started in February 2005 with an investment of [CNY](/wiki/CNY "CNY") 3.372 billion. International flights to neighboring Asian countries have also been enhanced, including direct flights to [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") and [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya"), Japan. Terminal 3 has been available for service since early 2017.

[Wuhan Hannan General Airport](/wiki/Wuhan_Hannan_General_Airport "Wuhan Hannan General Airport") is an airport dedicated to general aviation, located in [Hannan District](/wiki/Hannan_District "Hannan District") of Wuhan. It is the largest airport in China that only handles general aviation.[[182]](#cite_note-184) On December 1, 2017, construction began on [Wuhan Caidian General Airport](/wiki/Wuhan_Caidian_General_Airport "Wuhan Caidian General Airport"), another airport dedicated to general aviation, which is located in [Caidian District](/wiki/Caidian_District "Caidian District") of Wuhan.[[183]](#cite_note-185)

### Highways and expressways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Highways and expressways")]

Numerous major highways and expressways pass through Wuhan, including:

* [China National Highway 107](/wiki/China_National_Highway_107 "China National Highway 107")
* [China National Highway 316](/wiki/China_National_Highway_316 "China National Highway 316")
* [China National Highway 318](/wiki/China_National_Highway_318 "China National Highway 318")
* [G42 Shanghai–Chengdu Expressway](/wiki/G42_Shanghai%E2%80%93Chengdu_Expressway "G42 Shanghai–Chengdu Expressway")
* [G0422 Wuhan–Shenzhen Expressway](/wiki/G0422_Wuhan%E2%80%93Shenzhen_Expressway "G0422 Wuhan–Shenzhen Expressway")

### Bicycle-sharing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Bicycle-sharing system")]

As of May 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit), the Wuhan and [Hangzhou Public Bicycle](/wiki/Hangzhou_Public_Bicycle "Hangzhou Public Bicycle") bike-share systems in China were the largest in the world, with around 90,000 and 60,000 bicycles respectively.[[184]](#cite_note-Access2011-186) In 2012 the Wuhan and Hangzhou Public Bicycle programs in China are the largest in the world, with around 90,000 and 60,000 bicycles respectively. China has seen a rise in private "dockless" bike shares with fleets that dwarf systems in size outside China.[[185]](#cite_note-187) Initially, a number of traditional (third-generation) docked public bike systems operated by local municipal governments opened across China, with the largest ones being in Wuhan and Hangzhou. The first was introduced in Beijing in 2007. However, third-generation bike sharing is not considered successful for the majority cities in China. Bike sharing in Beijing virtually stopped and it also has encountered difficulties in Shanghai and Wuhan.[[186]](#cite_note-188)

## Destinations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Destinations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/ConcertGroupPano.jpg/960px-ConcertGroupPano.jpg)](/wiki/File:ConcertGroupPano.jpg "Replica instruments of ancient originals are played at the Hubei Provincial Museum. A replica set of bronze concert bells is in the background and a set of stone chimes is to the right.")

Replica instruments of ancient originals are played at the [Hubei Provincial Museum](/wiki/Hubei_Provincial_Museum "Hubei Provincial Museum").  
A replica set of bronze concert bells is in the background and a set of stone chimes is to the right.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E7%9C%81%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A6%86%E9%B8%9F%E7%9E%B0%E5%9B%BE.jpg/250px-%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E7%9C%81%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A6%86%E9%B8%9F%E7%9E%B0%E5%9B%BE.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E7%9C%81%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A6%86%E9%B8%9F%E7%9E%B0%E5%9B%BE.jpg)

[Hubei Provincial Museum](/wiki/Hubei_Provincial_Museum "Hubei Provincial Museum")

* The [Yellow Crane Tower](/wiki/Yellow_Crane_Tower "Yellow Crane Tower") (*Huanghelou*) is presumed to have been first built in approximately 220 AD. The tower has been destroyed and reconstructed numerous times, and was burned last according to some sources in 1884. The tower underwent complete reconstruction in 1981. The reconstruction utilized modern materials and added an elevator while maintaining the traditional design in the tower's outward appearance.
* [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang,_Hubei "Wuchang, Hubei") has the largest and second largest lakes within a city in China, the [East Lake](/wiki/East_Lake_(Wuhan) "East Lake (Wuhan)") and [Tangxun Lake](/wiki/Tangxun_Lake "Tangxun Lake"), as well as the [South Lake](/wiki/South_Lake_(Wuhan) "South Lake (Wuhan)"). East Lake in Wuhan is six times the size of the [West Lake](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake") in [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), Zhejiang province. The total area is more than 80 km2 (31 sq mi) of which the lake is covering an area of 33 km2 (13 sq mi). In the springtime, the shores of East Lake become a garden of flowers with the Mei blossoms as the king and the Cherry Blossom as the queen among the species at [East Lake Cherry Blossom Park](/wiki/East_Lake_Cherry_Blossom_Park "East Lake Cherry Blossom Park"). Another famous flower is the lotus. The lake has a long history and especially the Chu Kingdom is well represented around East Lake. Moreover, in the [Moshan](/wiki/Mount_Mo "Mount Mo") Botanic Garden there are many types of plum blossoms, as well as lotus flowers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8%E6%9B%BE%E4%BE%AF%E4%B9%99%E7%B7%A8%E9%90%98.jpg/250px-%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8%E6%9B%BE%E4%BE%AF%E4%B9%99%E7%B7%A8%E9%90%98.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8%E6%9B%BE%E4%BE%AF%E4%B9%99%E7%B7%A8%E9%90%98.jpg)

[Bianzhong of Marquis Yi of Zeng](/wiki/Bianzhong_of_Marquis_Yi_of_Zeng "Bianzhong of Marquis Yi of Zeng"), made in 433 BC, now on display at the [Hubei Provincial Museum](/wiki/Hubei_Provincial_Museum "Hubei Provincial Museum") in Wuhan

* The [Hubei Provincial Museum](/wiki/Hubei_Provincial_Museum "Hubei Provincial Museum"): With over 200,000 valued artifacts, this is one of the leading museums in China. Especially the artefacts from the tomb of [Marquis Yi of Zeng](/wiki/Marquis_Yi_of_Zeng "Marquis Yi of Zeng") (Zeng Hou Yi), who lived in the 5th century BC, is a world unique treasure. The bell chime of Marquis Yi of Zeng is a bronze instrument performed 2430 years ago in ancient China (Warring States Period), and was discovered in the [Tomb of Marquis Yi of Zeng](/wiki/Tomb_of_Marquis_Yi_of_Zeng "Tomb of Marquis Yi of Zeng") in [Suizhou](/wiki/Suizhou "Suizhou"), Hubei in 1978. The whole chime weighs 5 tons, can perfectly play sound which was heard 2430 years ago, and was considered "The Eighth Wonder of the World".
* The [Wuhan Museum](/wiki/Wuhan_Museum "Wuhan Museum") has a collection of more than 100,000 artifacts, including [ceramic](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic"), [bronze ware](/wiki/Bronze_ware "Bronze ware"), paintings and [calligraphy](/wiki/Calligraphy "Calligraphy"), [jade](/wiki/Jade "Jade"), [wood carving](/wiki/Wood_carving "Wood carving"), [enamel ware](/w/index.php?title=Enamel_ware&action=edit&redlink=1 "Enamel ware (page does not exist)"), seals and so on. As a modern comprehensive museum, Wuhan Museum has the function in cultural relic collection, academic reach, publicity and education, cultural exchange, and recreation and entertainment.

[[187]](#cite_note-189)

* [Happy Valley Wuhan](/wiki/Happy_Valley_Wuhan "Happy Valley Wuhan") is a [theme park](/wiki/Theme_park "Theme park") in [Hongshan District](/wiki/Hongshan_District,_Wuhan "Hongshan District, Wuhan"). Opened on April 29, 2012, it is the fifth installation of the [Happy Valley](/wiki/Happy_Valley_(amusement_parks) "Happy Valley (amusement parks)") theme park chain.[[188]](#cite_note-RCDB_Wuhan-190)
* The Rock and Bonsai Museum includes a mounted [platybelodon](/wiki/Platybelodon "Platybelodon") skeleton, many unique stones, a quartz crystal the size of an automobile, and an outdoor garden with miniature trees in the *[penjing](/wiki/Penjing "Penjing")* ("Chinese Bonsai") style.
* [Jiqing Street](/w/index.php?title=Jiqing_Street&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jiqing Street (page does not exist)") (吉庆街) holds many roadside restaurants and street performers during the evening and is the site of a Live Show with stories of events on this street by contemporary writer [Chi Li](/wiki/Chi_Li "Chi Li").
* The [Lute Platform](/wiki/Lute_Platform "Lute Platform") in [Hanyang](/wiki/Hanyang_District "Hanyang District") was where the legendary musician [Yu Boya](/wiki/Yu_Boya "Yu Boya") is said to have played. This is the birthplace of the renowned legend of seeking a soul mate through "high mountains and flowing water". According to the story behind the Chinese word '[知音](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%9F%A5%E9%9F%B3#Chinese "wikt:知音")' (*zhīyīn*; 'understanding music'), Yu Boya played for the last time over the grave of his friend [Zhong Ziqi](/w/index.php?title=Zhong_Ziqi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zhong Ziqi (page does not exist)"), then smashed his lute because the only person able to appreciate his music was dead.[[189]](#cite_note-武汉三大名胜之古琴台-191)
* [Mao's Villa](/w/index.php?title=Mao%27s_Villa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mao's Villa (page does not exist)") (毛澤東別墅), Mao Zedong's villa between 1960 and 1974; includes garden, living quarters, conference room, bomb shelter and swimming pool.[[190]](#cite_note-etg-192)[[191]](#cite_note-fodor-193)
* Some luxury [riverboat](/wiki/Riverboat "Riverboat") tours begin here after a flight from Beijing or Shanghai, with several days of flatland cruising and then climbing through the [Three Gorges](/wiki/Three_Gorges "Three Gorges") with passage upstream past the [Gezhouba](/wiki/Gezhouba_Dam "Gezhouba Dam") and [Three Gorges](/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam "Three Gorges Dam") dams to the city of [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"). With the completion of the dam, a number of cruises now start from the upstream side and continue west, with tourists traveling by motorcoach from Wuhan.
* [Wuying Pagoda](/wiki/Wuying_Pagoda "Wuying Pagoda") or the "Shadowless Pagoda" is the oldest standing architectural feature in Wuhan, dating from the closing days of the [Southern Song dynasty](/wiki/Southern_Song_dynasty "Southern Song dynasty").
* [Chu River and Han Street](/wiki/Chu_River_and_Han_Street "Chu River and Han Street"), a shopping district located in [Wuchang](/wiki/Wuchang_District "Wuchang District") with many tourist attractions, including Han Show theater, [Madame Tussauds](/wiki/Madame_Tussauds "Madame Tussauds") [wax museum](/wiki/Wax_museum "Wax museum"), and Movie Culture Park, etc. This project was initiated as a water connecting channel between [East Lake](/wiki/East_Lake_(Wuhan) "East Lake (Wuhan)") and [Shahu Lake](/w/index.php?title=Shahu_Lake&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shahu Lake (page does not exist)").
* [Wuhan Zoo](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan_Zoo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wuhan Zoo (page does not exist)") in Hanyang.[[192]](#cite_note-194)
* The [Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market](/wiki/Huanan_Seafood_Wholesale_Market "Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market") is a live animal and seafood market in the [Jianghan District](/wiki/Jianghan_District "Jianghan District"). The market is located in the newer part of the city near shops and apartment blocks and is close to [Hankou railway station](/wiki/Hankou_railway_station "Hankou railway station"). The market has been identified as a possible point of origin of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[193]](#cite_note-Hui_I_Azhar_Madani_Ntoumi_2020_pp._264–266-195)
* The [Wuhan Institute of Virology](/wiki/Wuhan_Institute_of_Virology "Wuhan Institute of Virology") (WIV) is located in the Wuchan District. It is, "the key laboratory of the Chinese Academy of Sciences for newly emerging and fulminating infectious disease pathogen and biosecurity."[[194]](#cite_note-196) The institute has been an active premier research center for the study of [coronaviruses](/wiki/Coronavirus "Coronavirus").[[195]](#cite_note-197)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Tanawin_mula_sa_Loob_ng_mga_Parke_ng_mga_Bato_sa_Wuhan_%28%E6%AD%A6%E6%B1%89%E5%A5%87%E7%9F%B3%E5%9B%AD-Wuhan_Stones_Park%29.jpg/960px-Tanawin_mula_sa_Loob_ng_mga_Parke_ng_mga_Bato_sa_Wuhan_%28%E6%AD%A6%E6%B1%89%E5%A5%87%E7%9F%B3%E5%9B%AD-Wuhan_Stones_Park%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tanawin_mula_sa_Loob_ng_mga_Parke_ng_mga_Bato_sa_Wuhan_(%E6%AD%A6%E6%B1%89%E5%A5%87%E7%9F%B3%E5%9B%AD-Wuhan_Stones_Park).jpg "View from within the Wuhan Stones Park (武汉奇石园) along Lumo Road.")

View from within the Wuhan Stones Park (武汉奇石园) along Lumo Road.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

### Schools and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Schools and universities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Whu_old_library_1.JPG/250px-Whu_old_library_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Whu_old_library_1.JPG)

The old library of [Wuhan University](/wiki/Wuhan_University "Wuhan University")

See also: [List of universities and colleges in Hubei](/wiki/List_of_universities_and_colleges_in_Hubei "List of universities and colleges in Hubei")

As of 2023, there are 82 higher educational institutions in Wuhan, making it a leading educational hub in the [Central China](/wiki/Central_China "Central China") region.[[196]](#cite_note-198) Wuhan is also an important hub for international students, and it was ranked the best city in the Central China region, 4th in China, and 98th globally by the QS Best Student Cities Rankings in 2023.[[197]](#cite_note-199) According to the [U.S. News & World Report Best Global University Ranking](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report_Best_Global_Universities_Ranking "U.S. News & World Report Best Global Universities Ranking") for 2025–26, Wuhan had 16 universities included in the rankings, with two universities in the top 100 and six in the top 500. It is the only city in the Central China region with two universities ranked in the top 100 globally, and it is one of only five cities in Asia, following Hong Kong, Beijing, Shanghai, and Singapore, to achieve this distinction.[[198]](#cite_note-:522-200)

Prominent institutions include [Huazhong University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Huazhong_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Huazhong University of Science and Technology") and [Wuhan University](/wiki/Wuhan_University "Wuhan University"). Three state-level development zones and many enterprise incubators are also significant in Wuhan's education and business development. Wuhan ranks third in China in overall strength of science and technology.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Hust-gc4a6ced49_1920.jpg/250px-Hust-gc4a6ced49_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hust-gc4a6ced49_1920.jpg)

[Huazhong University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Huazhong_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Huazhong University of Science and Technology")

[Huazhong University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Huazhong_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Huazhong University of Science and Technology") (HUST), located in the [Optics Valley of China](/wiki/Optics_Valley_of_China "Optics Valley of China") near East Lake, is a [Project 985](/wiki/Project_985 "Project 985") and Class A [Double First Class University](/wiki/Double_First-Class_Construction "Double First-Class Construction").[[200]](#cite_note-Chinese_Department_of_Education-202) HUST manages Wuhan National Laboratories for Opto-electronics (WNLO), which is one of the five national laboratories in China. HUST is also one of four Chinese universities eligible to run the national laboratory and the national major science and technology infrastructure. Founded in 1953 as Huazhong Institute of Technology, it combined with three other universities (including former Tongji Medical University founded in 1907) in 2000 to form the new HUST, and has 42 schools and departments covering 12 comprehensive disciplines.[[201]](#cite_note-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204) HUST has 12 Fellows of [Chinese Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Chinese_Academy_of_Sciences "Chinese Academy of Sciences") and 17 Fellows of [Chinese Academy of Engineering](/wiki/Chinese_Academy_of_Engineering "Chinese Academy of Engineering").[[203]](#cite_note-205) As of 2025, the [U.S. News](/wiki/U.S._News "U.S. News")' 2025 [U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/US_News_and_World_Report "US News and World Report") ranked HUST #91 in the world,[[204]](#cite_note-206) while the [Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities") ranked the university #73 in the world, 13th in Asia and 8th in China.[[205]](#cite_note-shanghairanking.com-207) More than 2,000 international students from 120 countries pursue degrees at HUST.[[206]](#cite_note-208)

[Wuhan University](/wiki/Wuhan_University "Wuhan University") is another [Project 985](/wiki/Project_985 "Project 985") and Class A [Double First Class University](/wiki/Double_First-Class_Construction "Double First-Class Construction"),[[200]](#cite_note-Chinese_Department_of_Education-202) which was ranked #81 in the world, #15 in Asia and 8th in China by the 2025 [Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")[[207]](#cite_note-209)[[205]](#cite_note-shanghairanking.com-207) and 90th in the world and 17th in Asia by the *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*,[[208]](#cite_note-:02-210) while it was ranked the 134th by the 2025 [Times Higher Education](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings");[[209]](#cite_note-211) established in 1893, the old Wuhan University absorbed three other schools (two of them being its spin-offs since the 1950s) in 2000 to become a university with 36 schools in 6 faculties. Since the 1950s it has received international students from more than 109 countries.[[210]](#cite_note-212)

As of the end of 2013, in Wuhan there were 1,024 kindergartens with 224,300 children, 590 primary schools with 424,000 students, 369 general high schools with 314,000 students, 105 secondary vocational and technical schools with 98,600 students, and 80 colleges and universities with 966,400 undergraduates and junior college students and 107,400 postgraduate students.[[211]](#cite_note-213) There are several [international schools in Wuhan](/wiki/List_of_international_schools_in_China#Wuhan "List of international schools in China").

### Scientific research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Scientific research")]

Wuhan is a major city in the world by scientific research outputs and it ranks [8th](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output "List of cities by scientific output") globally and 5th in the Asia-Pacific & China (after [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") and [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou")).[[212]](#cite_note-214) When compared to other countries in the region, Wuhan ranked higher than Australia, securing fifth place in Asia and Oceania after China, Japan, South Korea, and India, according to the Nature Index for 2025. For instance, Wuhan's share of the 2024 Nature Index is 1,526.22, while Australia's share is 1,472.49.[[213]](#cite_note-215)[[214]](#cite_note-216)

Wuhan contains three national development zones and four scientific and technological development parks, as well as numerous enterprise incubators, over 350 research institutes, 1470 high-tech enterprises, and over 400,000 experts and technicians.

Founded in 1958, the Wuhan Branch of [Chinese Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Chinese_Academy_of_Sciences "Chinese Academy of Sciences") is one of the twelve national branches of CAS. It is composed of 9 independent organizations, including the headquarters at Xiaohongshan, Wuchang. It has had a staff of 3,900, among which 8 are CAS fellows, and one is a [Chinese Academy of Engineering](/wiki/Chinese_Academy_of_Engineering "Chinese Academy of Engineering") fellow. As of 2013, the achievements gained by WHB had won 23 National Awards and 778 Provincial Awards.[[215]](#cite_note-217) Wuhan Research Institute of Post and Telecommunications (now known as FiberHome Technologies Group) is the national center for optical communication research in China, and is where the first [optical fiber](/wiki/Optical_fiber "Optical fiber") in the country was produced.[[216]](#cite_note-218) The [Wuhan Institute of Virology](/wiki/Wuhan_Institute_of_Virology "Wuhan Institute of Virology") is also operated by the CAS.

[Wuhan University of Technology](/wiki/Wuhan_University_of_Technology "Wuhan University of Technology") is another major national university with three main campuses located in the [Wuchang District](/wiki/Wuchang_District "Wuchang District"). Founded in the year 2000, it was merged from three major universities, Wuhan University of Technology (established in 1948), Wuhan Transportation University (established in 1946) and Wuhan Automotive Polytechnic University (established in 1958). Wuhan University of Technology, together with [China University of Geosciences (Wuhan)](/wiki/China_University_of_Geosciences_(Wuhan) "China University of Geosciences (Wuhan)"), [Huazhong Agricultural University](/wiki/Huazhong_Agricultural_University "Huazhong Agricultural University"), [Zhongnan University of Economics and Law](/wiki/Zhongnan_University_of_Economics_and_Law "Zhongnan University of Economics and Law") and [Central China Normal University](/wiki/Central_China_Normal_University "Central China Normal University") (or [Huazhong Normal University](/wiki/Huazhong_Normal_University "Huazhong Normal University")), are the leading Chinese universities accredited by the Ministry of Education under the "State [Project 211](/wiki/Project_211 "Project 211")" for [Chinese higher education institutions](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_China "List of universities in China"). Other major [research universities](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") have its seat in the city, including [Wuhan University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Wuhan_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Wuhan University of Science and Technology"), [Hubei University](/wiki/Hubei_University "Hubei University"), [Hubei University of Technology](/wiki/Hubei_University_of_Technology "Hubei University of Technology"), and [South Central University for Nationalities](/wiki/South_Central_University_for_Nationalities "South Central University for Nationalities").[[217]](#cite_note-:2-219)[[218]](#cite_note-220)

Wuhan is a major site for water planning and research in central China.[[219]](#cite_note-:05-221): 117

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Media")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/%E9%BE%9F%E5%B1%B1%E7%94%B5%E8%A7%86%E5%A1%94.jpg/250px-%E9%BE%9F%E5%B1%B1%E7%94%B5%E8%A7%86%E5%A1%94.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E9%BE%9F%E5%B1%B1%E7%94%B5%E8%A7%86%E5%A1%94.jpg)

Tortoise Mountain TV Tower

The headquarters of [Hubei Television](/wiki/Hubei_Television "Hubei Television") is located in Wuchang District. [Tortoise Mountain TV Tower](/wiki/Tortoise_Mountain_TV_Tower "Tortoise Mountain TV Tower") is China's first self-developed TV tower, opened in 1986. The modern newspapers in Wuhan can be dated back to 1866, when *Hankow Times*, a newspaper in English, was founded. Before 1949, more than 50 newspapers and magazines were published by foreigners in Wuhan. *Chao-wen Hsin-pao*, founded by Ai Xiaomei in 1873, was the first Chinese newspaper to appear in Hankou (one of the cities that was merged into Wuhan). During the [Northern Expedition](/wiki/Northern_Expedition "Northern Expedition") era (1926–1928), journalism in Wuhan came to a climax; more than 120 newspapers and periodicals, including national newspapers such as *[Central Daily News](/wiki/Central_Daily_News "Central Daily News")* and *[Republican Daily News](/wiki/Republican_Daily_News "Republican Daily News")*, were founded or published during this time.[[220]](#cite_note-222) *Chutian Metropolis Daily* and *Wuhan Evening News* are two major local commercial tabloid newspapers. Both of them have entered the list of 100 most widely circulated newspapers of the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Culture")]

The plum blossom is the city's emblem, chosen partly because of the long history of local plum cultivation and use, and partly to recognize the plum's current economic significance in terms of cultivation and research. Local wild plums were used medicinally during the [Qin](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") and [Han](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") dynasties. Cultivation of the fruit began during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"). Some traditional new year customs revolve around the planting of plums.

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Language")]

Main article: [Wuhan dialect](/wiki/Wuhan_dialect "Wuhan dialect")

Wuhan natives speak a variety of [Southwestern Mandarin](/wiki/Southwestern_Mandarin "Southwestern Mandarin") Chinese referred to as Wuhan dialect that differs slightly between the districts of Wuhan, including Wuchang dialect in Wuchang District, Hankou dialect in the Hankou districts, Hanyang dialect in Hanyang District, and Qingshan dialect in Qingshan District.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Hot_Dry_Noodles.jpg/250px-Hot_Dry_Noodles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hot_Dry_Noodles.jpg)

Hot Dry Noodles

Main article: [Hubei cuisine](/wiki/Hubei_cuisine "Hubei cuisine")

Hubei cuisine is one of [China's ten major styles of cooking](/wiki/Chinese_regional_cuisine "Chinese regional cuisine"). With a history of more than 2,000 years, Hubei cuisine, originating in ancient Chu cuisine, has developed a number of distinctive dishes, such as steamed blunt-snout bream in clear soup, preserved ham with flowering Chinese cabbage, and others. On the third day of the third month of the lunar calendar, many in Wuhan eat *dìcài zhǔ jīdàn* (地菜煮鸡蛋), an egg dish which is supposed to prevent illness in the coming year.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Pumpkin_tangyuan_%28%E6%B1%A4%E5%9C%86%29_with_red_bean_baste_and_black_sesame_fillings.jpg/250px-Pumpkin_tangyuan_%28%E6%B1%A4%E5%9C%86%29_with_red_bean_baste_and_black_sesame_fillings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pumpkin_tangyuan_(%E6%B1%A4%E5%9C%86)_with_red_bean_baste_and_black_sesame_fillings.jpg)

Tangyuan made from [glutinous rice flour](/wiki/Glutinous_rice_flour "Glutinous rice flour"), filled with [black sesame](/wiki/Sesame "Sesame") (黑芝麻) paste

"No need to be particular about the recipes; all foods have their own uses. [Rice wine](/wiki/Rice_wine "Rice wine") and [tangyuan](/wiki/Tangyuan_(food) "Tangyuan (food)") are excellent midnight snacks, while fat bream and flowering Chinese cabbages are great delicacies."[[222]](#cite_note-224) This attitude expressed in *Hankou Zhuzhici* reflects indirectly the eating habits and a wide variety of distinctive snacks with a long history in Wuhan, such as Qingshuizong (a pyramid-shaped dumpling made of glutinous rice wrapped in bamboo or reed leaves) in the Period of the Warring States, Chunbinbian in Northern and Southern dynasties, mung bean jelly in the Sui dynasty, youguo (a deep-fried twisted dough stick) in the Song and Yuan dynasties, rice wine and mianwo in the Ming and Qing dynasties, as well as three-delicacy stuffed skin of bean milk,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] tangbao (steamed dumpling filled with minced meat and gravy) and hot braised noodles (reganmian) in modern times.

*Guozao* ([過早](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%81%8E%E6%97%A9#Traditional_Chinese "wikt:過早")) is a popular way to say 'having breakfast' in Wuhan, and a part of the city's culture. As a hub for land transport in China, Wuhan has gathered and mixed together various habits and customs from neighboring cities and provinces in all directions, which gives rise to a concentration of diverse cuisines from different places. The most famous place to *guozao* (have breakfast) is Hubu Street (戶部巷), a 150-meter-long street in the neighborhood of Simenkou (司门口). Along its short length one can find nearly all the traditional foods of Wuhan, such as:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Mianwo.JPG/250px-Mianwo.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mianwo.JPG)

[Mianwo](/wiki/Mianwo "Mianwo"), a donut-shaped snack from Wuhan

* [Hot and dry noodles](/wiki/Re_gan_mian "Re gan mian"), *re-gan mian* ([热干面](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%83%AD%E5%B9%B2%E9%9D%A2#Simplified_Chinese "wikt:热干面")), consists of long freshly boiled noodles mixed with sesame paste. It is considered to be the most typical local food for breakfast.
* [Duck's neck](/wiki/Wuhan_duck "Wuhan duck") or *Ya Bozi* (鸭脖子) is a local version of this popular Chinese dish, made of duck necks and spices.
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Freshly_made_Doupi.jpeg/250px-Freshly_made_Doupi.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Freshly_made_Doupi.jpeg)

  Freshly made Doupi in Wuhan

  Bean skin or *[doupi](/wiki/Doupi "Doupi")* (豆皮) is a local dish with a filling of egg, rice, beef, mushrooms and beans cooked between two large round soybean skins and cut into pieces, structurally like a stuffed pizza without enclosing edges.
* [Soup dumpling](/wiki/Xiaolongbao#Related_varieties "Xiaolongbao") or *xiaolongtangbao* (小笼汤包) is a kind of dumpling with thin skin made of flour, steamed with very juicy meat inside, hence the name: *tang* (soup) *bao* (bun) – every time one takes a bite from it the "soup" inside is liable to spill out.
* A salty doughnut or *[mianwo](/wiki/Mianwo "Mianwo")* (麪窩) is a kind of savory donut with a salty taste. It is much thinner than a common donut and is a typical Wuhan local food.
* Shaomai wrapped in oil cake (油饼包烧麦): 1 oil cake is filled with 4 pieces of heavy oil siomai, and the heavy oil is required to put diced meat, mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and black pepper in it.
* Paste Soup Noodles (糊汤粉): It is a snack variety that uses round rice noodles as the main ingredient, fish paste soup, small shrimp, and chopped green onion as accessories.

### Opera

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Opera")]

[Han opera](/wiki/Han_opera "Han opera"), which is the local opera of Wuhan area, was one of China's oldest and most popular operas. During the late Qing dynasty, Han opera, blended with Hui opera, gave birth to Peking opera, the most popular opera in modern China. Thus Han opera has been called the "mother of Peking opera".[[223]](#cite_note-225)[[224]](#cite_note-226)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Wuhan_Sport_Centre_01.jpg/250px-Wuhan_Sport_Centre_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wuhan_Sport_Centre_01.jpg)

[Wuhan Sports Center](/wiki/Wuhan_Sports_Center "Wuhan Sports Center")

Wuhan had a professional football team, [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan_F.C. "Wuhan F.C."), that plays in the [China League One](/wiki/China_League_One "China League One"). Xinhua Road Sport Center, the team's home stadium, with a capacity of 32,137, is located in the heart of the city next to Zhongshan Park. For the 2013 season, Wuhan Zall was promoted to the top-tier league of Chinese football, [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League"), and relocated its home to [Wuhan Sports Center Stadium](/wiki/Wuhan_Sports_Center_Stadium "Wuhan Sports Center Stadium"), a modern stadium with 54,357 seats located in the suburbs of the city. However, the team did not play well in the ensuing season and was demoted back to China League One as the 2013 season ended. For financial and transportation reasons, the team moved back to Xinhua Road Sport Center in 2014. In January 2023, the team folded. Wuhan also has the [Wuhan Three Towns](/wiki/Wuhan_Three_Towns_F.C. "Wuhan Three Towns F.C.") in the Chinese Super League, who won the title during the 2022 season for the first time upon promotion from China League One.

The [Wuhan Gators](/wiki/Wuhan_Gators "Wuhan Gators") were a professional [arena football](/wiki/Arena_football "Arena football") team based in Wuhan. They were members of the [China Arena Football League](/wiki/China_Arena_Football_League "China Arena Football League") (CAFL).[[225]](#cite_note-227)

The 13,000-seat [Wuhan Sports Center Gymnasium](/wiki/Wuhan_Sports_Center_Gymnasium "Wuhan Sports Center Gymnasium") held the [2011 FIBA Asia Championship](/wiki/2011_FIBA_Asia_Championship "2011 FIBA Asia Championship") and was one of the venues for the [2019 FIBA Basketball World Cup](/wiki/2019_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2019 FIBA Basketball World Cup").[[226]](#cite_note-fiba.com-228) The 7th [Military World Games](/wiki/Military_World_Games "Military World Games") were hosted in Wuhan from October 18 to 27, 2019.[[227]](#cite_note-229)[[228]](#cite_note-230)

The city has been the venue for the women's tennis tournament, the [Wuhan Open](/wiki/Wuhan_Open "Wuhan Open"), one of the [WTA's](/wiki/WTA_Tier_I_tournaments "WTA Tier I tournaments") [Premier 5 tournaments](/wiki/WTA_Premier_tournaments "WTA Premier tournaments"), since 2014.

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Architecture")]

### Bridges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Bridges")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Wuhan_Erqi_Yangtze_River_Bridge.jpg/250px-Wuhan_Erqi_Yangtze_River_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wuhan_Erqi_Yangtze_River_Bridge.jpg)

Wuhan Erqi Yangtze River Bridge

Wuhan has [eleven bridges and one tunnel across the Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River_bridges_and_tunnels "Yangtze River bridges and tunnels"). The [Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge"), also called the First Bridge, was built over the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") in 1957, carrying a railroad directly across the river between hills known as Snake Hill and Turtle Hill. Before this bridge was built it could take up to an entire day to [barge](/wiki/Train_ferry "Train ferry") railcars across. Including its approaches, it is 5,511 feet (1,680 m) long, and it accommodates both a double-track railway on a lower deck and a four-lane roadway above. It was built with the assistance of advisers from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").

The [Second Bridge](/wiki/Second_Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Second Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge"), a [cable-stayed bridge](/wiki/Cable-stayed_bridge "Cable-stayed bridge") built of [prestressed concrete](/wiki/Prestressed_concrete "Prestressed concrete"), has a central span of 400 meters (1,300 feet); it is 4,678 meters (15,348 feet) in length (including 1,877 meters (6,158 feet) of the main bridge) and 26.5 to 33.5 meters (86.9 to 109.9 feet) in width. Its main bridgeheads are 90 meters (300 feet) high each, pulling 392 thick slanting cables together in the shape of double fans so that the central span of the bridge is well poised on the piers and the bridge's stability and vibration resistance are ensured. With six lanes on the deck, the bridge is designed to handle the daily passage of 50,000 motor vehicles. The bridge was completed in 1995.

The [Third Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Third_Wuhan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Third Wuhan Yangtze River Bridge"), also called Baishazhou Bridge, was completed in September 2000. Located 8.6 kilometers (5.3 miles) southwest of the First Bridge, construction of Baishazhou Bridge started in 1997. With an investment of over 1.4 billion yuan (about [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")170,000,000), the bridge, which is 3,586 meters (11,765 feet) long and 26.5 meters (86.9 feet) wide, has six lanes and has a capacity of 50,000 vehicles a day. The bridge is expected to serve as a major passage for the future Wuhan Ring Road, greatly easing the city's traffic and aiding local economic development.

The [Yangluo Bridge](/wiki/Yangluo_Bridge "Yangluo Bridge") carries [Wuhan's Ring Road](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan_Ring_Road&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wuhan Ring Road (page does not exist)") across the Yangtze in the city's eastern suburbs (connecting the [Hongshan District](/wiki/Hongshan_District,_Wuhan "Hongshan District, Wuhan") with the [Xinzhou District](/wiki/Xinzhou_District,_Wuhan "Xinzhou District, Wuhan")). It was opened on December 26, 2007.

The [Wuhan Tianxingzhou Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Wuhan_Tianxingzhou_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhan Tianxingzhou Yangtze River Bridge") crosses the Yangtze in the northeastern part of the city, downstream of the Second bridge. It is named after Tianxing Island (*Tianxingzhou*), above which it crosses the river. Built at a cost of 11 billion yuan, the 4,657-meter cable suspension bridge was opened on December 26, 2009,[[229]](#cite_note-231) in time for the opening of the [Wuhan railway station](/wiki/Wuhan_railway_station "Wuhan railway station"). It is a combined road and rail bridge, and carries the [Wuhan–Guangzhou high-speed railway](/wiki/Wuhan%E2%80%93Guangzhou_high-speed_railway "Wuhan–Guangzhou high-speed railway") across the river.

### Skyscrapers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Skyscrapers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Wuhan_Greenland_Center.jpg/250px-Wuhan_Greenland_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wuhan_Greenland_Center.jpg)

[Wuhan Greenland Center](/wiki/Wuhan_Greenland_Center "Wuhan Greenland Center"), the tallest building in Wuhan since 2023

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Wuhan](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Wuhan "List of tallest buildings in Wuhan")

The [Yellow Crane Tower](/wiki/Yellow_Crane_Tower "Yellow Crane Tower"), historically one of the tallest buildings in Wuhan, is considered one of the [Four Great Towers of China](/wiki/Four_Great_Towers_of_China "Four Great Towers of China") and was destroyed twelve times, both by warfare and by fire. The tower is classified as an [AAAAA scenic area](/wiki/AAAAA_scenic_area "AAAAA scenic area") by the [China National Tourism Administration](/wiki/China_National_Tourism_Administration "China National Tourism Administration").[[230]](#cite_note-232) At 475.6 meters (1,560 ft) in height, the [Wuhan Greenland Center](/wiki/Wuhan_Greenland_Center "Wuhan Greenland Center") is the tallest skyscraper in Wuhan and in Central China, as well as the [eighth tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China "List of tallest buildings in China").[[231]](#cite_note-233)

The [Wuhan Center](/wiki/Wuhan_Center "Wuhan Center"), the second tallest skyscraper in Wuhan, was the tallest building in the city when it was completed in 2019. It retained the title until Wuhan Greenland Center surpassed it in 2023.[[232]](#cite_note-skyscraper-center-234) [Riverview Plaza](/wiki/Riverview_Plaza "Riverview Plaza") is a 376 meters (1,234 ft) tall skyscraper located in Wuhan. It was completed in 2021 and is currently the third tallest building in the city. The [Phoenix Towers](/wiki/Phoenix_Towers_(China) "Phoenix Towers (China)") are proposed [supertall skyscrapers](/wiki/Supertall_skyscraper "Supertall skyscraper") planned for construction in Wuhan. At 1 kilometer (3,300 ft) high, the towers would be among the tallest structures in the world when completed.[[233]](#cite_note-Zhang-235)

### Former Twinnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Former Twinnings")]

The city of Arnhem has unilaterally ended its twinning with Wuhan on July 21, 2021, citing concerns about the [persecution of Uyghurs in China](/wiki/Persecution_of_Uyghurs_in_China "Persecution of Uyghurs in China").[[251]](#cite_note-253)

| City | Country | From | Until |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Arnhem](/wiki/Arnhem "Arnhem") | Netherlands | September 6, 1999 | July 21, 2021 |

## Nature and wildlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wuhan&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Nature and wildlife")]

In [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology"), the [Baiji](/wiki/Baiji "Baiji") ("Yangtze River dolphin") has many origin stories. In one legend, the Baiji was the daughter of a general who was deported from the city of Wuhan during a war. During his duty, the daughter ran away. Later, the general met a woman who told him how her father was a general, and when he realized that she was his daughter, he threw himself into the river out of shame. The daughter ran after him and also fell into the river. Before they were drowned, the daughter was transformed into a dolphin, and the general a porpoise.[[252]](#cite_note-turvy-254)
